in the field of [language](/wiki/Language "Language") is the [communication](/wiki/Communication "Communication") of the [meaning](/wiki/Semantics "Semantics") of a [source-language](#Source_and_target_languages) text by means of an [equivalent](/wiki/Dynamic_and_formal_equivalence "Dynamic and formal equivalence") [target-language](#Source_and_target_languages) text (also called the 'receptor language').[[1]](#cite_note-1) The English language draws a [terminological](/wiki/Terminological "Terminological") distinction (which does not exist in every language) between *translating*, which refers to written texts, and *[interpreting](/wiki/Interpreting "Interpreting")*, which denotes the oral or [signed](/wiki/Sign_language "Sign language") rendering of speech between languages.

**Translation** in the field of [language](/wiki/Language "Language") is the [communication](/wiki/Communication "Communication") of the [meaning](/wiki/Semantics "Semantics") of a [source-language](#Source_and_target_languages) text by means of an [equivalent](/wiki/Dynamic_and_formal_equivalence "Dynamic and formal equivalence") [target-language](#Source_and_target_languages) text (also called the 'receptor language').[[1]](#cite_note-1) The English language draws a [terminological](/wiki/Terminological "Terminological") distinction (which does not exist in every language) between *translating*, which refers to written texts, and *[interpreting](/wiki/Interpreting "Interpreting")*, which denotes the oral or [signed](/wiki/Sign_language "Sign language") rendering of speech between languages.

A translator always risks inadvertently introducing source-language words, [grammar](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar"), or [syntax](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax") into the target-language rendering. On the other hand, such "spill-overs" have sometimes imported useful source-language [calques](/wiki/Calque "Calque") and [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") that have enriched target languages.

Because of the laboriousness of the translation process, since the 1940s efforts have been made, with varying degrees of success, to [automate translation](/wiki/Machine_translation "Machine translation") or to [mechanically aid the human translator](/wiki/Computer-assisted_translation "Computer-assisted translation").[[2]](#cite_note-2) More recently, the rise of the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") has fostered a [world-wide market](/wiki/World-wide_market "World-wide market") for [translation services](/wiki/Translation_services "Translation services") and has facilitated "[language localisation](/wiki/Language_localisation "Language localisation")".[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Rosetta_Stone_BW.jpeg/250px-Rosetta_Stone_BW.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Rosetta_Stone_BW.jpeg)

The [Rosetta Stone](/wiki/Rosetta_Stone "Rosetta Stone"), a symbol of the art of translation[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The word for the [concept](/wiki/Concept "Concept") of "translation", in English and some other European languages, stems from the Latin [noun](/wiki/Noun "Noun") *translatio*,[[5]](#cite_note-5) formed from the [adverb](/wiki/Adverb "Adverb") *trans*, "across", and *-latio*, derived from *latus*, the [past participle](/wiki/Past_participle "Past participle") of the [verb](/wiki/Verb "Verb") *ferre*, to "carry" or "bring". Thus, the Latin noun *translatio* and its [cognate](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") modern derivatives mean the "bringing across" (i.e., the *transferring*) of a text from one language to another.[[6]](#cite_note-The_Translator_p._83-6)

In some other European languages, the word for the [concept](/wiki/Concept "Concept") of "translation" stems from another Latin [noun](/wiki/Noun "Noun"), *trāductiō*, derived from the [verb](/wiki/Verb "Verb") *[trādūcō](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/traduco#Latin "wikt:traduco")*, "bring across", formed from the [adverb](/wiki/Adverb "Adverb") *trans*, "across", and *dūcō*, to "lead" or "bring".[[6]](#cite_note-The_Translator_p._83-6)

The [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") term for "translation" (*metaphrasis*, "a speaking across") has supplied English with "[metaphrase](/wiki/Metaphrase "Metaphrase")" (word-for-word translation), as contrasted with "[paraphrase](/wiki/Paraphrase "Paraphrase")" (rephrasing in other words, from *paraphrasis*).[[6]](#cite_note-The_Translator_p._83-6) "Metaphrase" corresponds in one of the more recent terminologies to [formal equivalence](#Equivalence), and "paraphrase" to [dynamic equivalence](#Equivalence).[[7]](#cite_note-Kasparek_p._84-7)

The concept of metaphrase (i.e., word-for-word translation) is an imperfect concept, because a given word in a given language often carries more than one meaning, and because a similar given meaning may often be represented in a given language by more than one word. Nevertheless, metaphrase and paraphrase may be useful as ideal concepts that mark the extremes in the spectrum of possible approaches to translation.

See also the entry for *[translation](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/translation#English "wikt:translation")* at Wiktionary.

## Theories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Theories")]

### Western theory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Western theory")]

Discussions of the theory and practice of translation reach back into [antiquity](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history") and show remarkable continuities. The [ancient Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greeks "Ancient Greeks") distinguished between *metaphrase* (literal translation) and *paraphrase*. This distinction was adopted by English poet and translator [John Dryden](/wiki/John_Dryden "John Dryden") (1631–1700), who described translation as the judicious blending of these two modes of phrasing when selecting, in the target language, "counterparts," or [equivalents](/wiki/Dynamic_and_formal_equivalence "Dynamic and formal equivalence"), for the expressions used in the source language:

> When [words] appear... literally graceful, it were an injury to the author that they should be changed. But since... what is beautiful in one [language] is often barbarous, nay sometimes nonsense, in another, it would be unreasonable to limit a translator to the narrow compass of his author's words: 'tis enough if he choose out some expression which does not vitiate the sense.[[6]](#cite_note-The_Translator_p._83-6)

Dryden cautioned, however, against the licence of "imitation", i.e. of adapted translation: "When a painter copies from the life... he has no privilege to alter features and lineaments..."[[7]](#cite_note-Kasparek_p._84-7)

This general formulation of the central concept of translation—[equivalence](/wiki/Dynamic_and_formal_equivalence "Dynamic and formal equivalence")—is as adequate as any that has been proposed since [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero") and [Horace](/wiki/Horace "Horace"), who, in 1st-century-BCE [Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), famously and literally cautioned against translating word for word (*verbum pro verbo*).[[7]](#cite_note-Kasparek_p._84-7)

Despite occasional theoretical diversity, the actual *practice* of translation has hardly changed since antiquity. Except for some extreme metaphrasers in the early Christian period and the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and adapters in various periods (especially pre-Classical Rome, and the 18th century), translators have generally shown prudent flexibility in seeking [equivalents](/wiki/Dynamic_and_formal_equivalence "Dynamic and formal equivalence")—"literal" where possible, paraphrastic where necessary—for the original [meaning](/wiki/Meaning_(linguistics) "Meaning (linguistics)") and other crucial "values" (e.g., [style](/wiki/Style_(fiction) "Style (fiction)"), [verse form](/wiki/Verse_form "Verse form"), concordance with musical accompaniment or, in films, with speech [articulatory](/wiki/Manner_of_articulation "Manner of articulation") movements) as determined from context.[[7]](#cite_note-Kasparek_p._84-7)

In general translators have sought to preserve the [context](/wiki/Context_(language_use) "Context (language use)") itself by reproducing the original order of [sememes](/wiki/Sememe "Sememe"), and hence [word order](/wiki/Word_order "Word order")[[8]](#cite_note-8)—when necessary, reinterpreting the actual [grammatical](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar") structure, for example, by shifting from [active](/wiki/Active_voice "Active voice") to [passive voice](/wiki/Passive_voice "Passive voice"), or *vice versa*. The grammatical differences between "fixed-word-order" [languages](/wiki/Language "Language")[[9]](#cite_note-9) (e.g. English, [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [German](/wiki/German_language "German language")) and "free-word-order" languages[[10]](#cite_note-10) (e.g., [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"), [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language")) have been no impediment in this regard.[[7]](#cite_note-Kasparek_p._84-7) The particular syntax (sentence-structure) characteristics of the text of a source language are adjusted to the syntactic requirements of the target language.

When a target language has lacked [terms](/wiki/Terminology "Terminology") that are found in a source language, translators have borrowed those terms, thereby enriching the target language. Thanks in great measure to the exchange of calques and loanwords between languages, and to their importation from other languages, there are few [concepts](/wiki/Concept "Concept") that are "[untranslatable](/wiki/Untranslatable "Untranslatable")" among the modern European languages.[[7]](#cite_note-Kasparek_p._84-7) A greater problem, however, is translating terms relating to cultural concepts that have no equivalent in the target language.[[11]](#cite_note-11) For full comprehension, such situations require the provision of a [gloss](/wiki/Gloss_(annotation)#In_linguistics "Gloss (annotation)").

Generally, the greater the contact and exchange that have existed between two languages, or between those languages and a third one, the greater is the ratio of [metaphrase](/wiki/Metaphrase "Metaphrase") to [paraphrase](/wiki/Paraphrase "Paraphrase") that may be used in translating among them. However, due to shifts in [ecological niches](/wiki/Ecological_niche "Ecological niche") of words, a common [etymology](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology") is sometimes misleading as a guide to current meaning in one or the other language. For example, the English *actual* should not be confused with the [cognate](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") French *actuel* ("present", "current"), the Polish *aktualny* ("present", "current," "topical", "timely", "feasible"),[[12]](#cite_note-Kasparek_p._85-12) the Swedish *aktuell* ("topical", "presently of importance"), the Russian актуальный ("urgent", "topical") or the Dutch *actueel* ("current").

The translator's role as a bridge for "carrying across" values between cultures has been discussed at least since [Terence](/wiki/Terence "Terence"), the 2nd-century-BCE Roman adapter of Greek comedies. The translator's role is, however, by no means a passive, mechanical one, and so has also been compared to that of an [artist](/wiki/Artist "Artist"). The main ground seems to be the concept of parallel creation found in critics such as [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero"). Dryden observed that "Translation is a type of drawing after life..." Comparison of the translator with a musician or actor goes back at least to [Samuel Johnson](/wiki/Samuel_Johnson "Samuel Johnson")'s remark about [Alexander Pope](/wiki/Alexander_Pope "Alexander Pope") playing [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer") on a [flageolet](/wiki/Flageolet "Flageolet"), while Homer himself used a [bassoon](/wiki/Bassoon "Bassoon").[[12]](#cite_note-Kasparek_p._85-12)

The translator of the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") into German, [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") (1483–1546), is credited with being the first European to posit that one translates satisfactorily only toward his own language. L.G. Kelly states that since [Johann Gottfried Herder](/wiki/Johann_Gottfried_Herder "Johann Gottfried Herder") in the 18th century "it has been axiomatic" that one translates only toward his own language.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Compounding the demands on the translator is the fact that no [dictionary](/wiki/Dictionary "Dictionary") or [thesaurus](/wiki/Thesaurus "Thesaurus") can ever be a fully adequate guide in translating. The Scottish historian [Alexander Tytler](/wiki/Alexander_Tytler "Alexander Tytler"), in his *Essay on the Principles of Translation* (1790), emphasized that assiduous reading is a more comprehensive guide to a language than are dictionaries. The same point, but also including listening to the [*spoken* language](/wiki/Spoken_language "Spoken language"), had earlier, in 1783, been made by the Polish poet and [grammarian](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar") [Onufry Kopczyński](/wiki/Onufry_Kopczy%C5%84ski "Onufry Kopczyński").[[14]](#cite_note-Kasparek_p._86-14)

### Other traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Other traditions")]

Due to [Western colonialism](/wiki/Western_colonialism "Western colonialism") and cultural dominance in recent centuries, Western translation traditions have largely replaced other traditions. The Western traditions draw on both ancient and medieval traditions, and on more recent European innovations.

Though earlier approaches to translation are less commonly used today, they retain importance when dealing with their products, as when historians view ancient or medieval records to piece together events which took place in non-Western or pre-Western environments. Also, though heavily influenced by Western traditions and practiced by translators taught in Western-style educational systems, Chinese and related translation traditions retain some theories and philosophies unique to the Chinese tradition.

#### Near East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Near East")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=). *(March 2012)* |

Traditions of translating material among the languages of ancient [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), [Assyria](/wiki/Assyria "Assyria") ([Syriac language](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language")), [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia"), and [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") ([Hebrew language](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language")) go back several millennia. There exist partial translations of the Sumerian *[Epic of Gilgamesh](/wiki/Epic_of_Gilgamesh "Epic of Gilgamesh")* (c. 2000 BCE) into [Southwest Asian](/wiki/Southwest_Asia "Southwest Asia") languages of the second millennium BCE.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

An early example of a [bilingual](/wiki/Bilingual "Bilingual") document is the 1274 BCE [Treaty of Kadesh](/wiki/Treaty_of_Kadesh "Treaty of Kadesh") between the [ancient Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") and [Hittie empires](/wiki/Hittite_Empire "Hittite Empire").

The [Babylonians](/wiki/Babylonia "Babylonia") were the first to establish translation as a profession.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The first translations of Greek and Coptic texts into Arabic, possibly indirectly from Syriac translations,[[17]](#cite_note-17) seem to have been undertaken as early as the late seventh century CE.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The second Abbasid Caliph funded a translation bureau in Baghdad in the eighth century.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Bayt al-Hikma, the famous library in Baghdad, was generously endowed and the collection included books in many languages, and it became a leading centre for the translation of works from antiquity into Arabic, with its own Translation Department.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Translations into European languages from Arabic versions of lost Greek and Roman texts began in the middle of the eleventh century, when the benefits to be gained from the Arabs’ knowledge of the classical texts were recognised by European scholars, particularly after the establishment of the Escuela de Traductores de Toledo in Spain.

[William Caxton](/wiki/William_Caxton "William Caxton")’s *Dictes or Sayengis of the Philosophres* (Sayings of the Philosophers, 1477) was a translation into English of an eleventh-century Egyptian text which reached English via translation into Latin and then French.

The translation of foreign works for publishing in Arabic was revived by the establishment of the [Madrasat al-Alsun](/wiki/Madrasat_al-Alsun "Madrasat al-Alsun") (School of Tongues) in Egypt in 1813.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

#### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Asia")]

Further information: [Chinese translation theory](/wiki/Chinese_translation_theory "Chinese translation theory")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Jingangjing.jpg/330px-Jingangjing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jingangjing.jpg)

[Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") *[Diamond Sutra](/wiki/Diamond_Sutra "Diamond Sutra")*, translated into [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") by [Kumārajīva](/wiki/Kum%C4%81raj%C4%ABva "Kumārajīva") – world's oldest known dated printed book (868 CE)

There is a separate tradition of translation in [South](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), [Southeast](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") (primarily of texts from the [Indian](/wiki/India "India") and [Chinese](/wiki/China "China") civilizations), connected especially with the rendering of religious, particularly [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist"), texts and with the governance of the Chinese empire. Classical Indian translation is characterized by loose adaptation, rather than the closer translation more commonly found in Europe; and [Chinese translation theory](/wiki/Chinese_translation_theory "Chinese translation theory") identifies various criteria and limitations in translation.

In the East Asian sphere of Chinese cultural influence, more important than translation *per se* has been the use and reading of Chinese texts, which also had substantial influence on the Japanese, Korean and Vietnamese languages, with substantial [borrowings of Chinese vocabulary](/wiki/Sino-Xenic_vocabularies "Sino-Xenic vocabularies") and writing system. Notable is the Japanese [kanbun](/wiki/Kanbun "Kanbun"), a system for [glossing](/wiki/Glossing "Glossing") Chinese texts for Japanese speakers.

Though Indianized states in Southeast Asia often translated [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") material into the local languages, the literate elites and scribes more commonly used Sanskrit as their primary language of culture and government.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/VOA_Perry_Link.jpg/330px-VOA_Perry_Link.jpg)](/wiki/File:VOA_Perry_Link.jpg)

[Perry Link](/wiki/Perry_Link "Perry Link")

Some special aspects of translating from [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") are illustrated in [Perry Link](/wiki/Perry_Link "Perry Link")'s discussion of translating the work of the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") poet [Wang Wei](/wiki/Wang_Wei_(Tang_dynasty) "Wang Wei (Tang dynasty)") (699–759 CE).[[22]](#cite_note-22)

> Some of the art of classical [Chinese poetry](/wiki/Chinese_poetry "Chinese poetry") [writes Link] must simply be set aside as [untranslatable](/wiki/Untranslatable "Untranslatable"). The internal structure of [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters") has a beauty of its own, and the [calligraphy](/wiki/Calligraphy "Calligraphy") in which classical poems were written is another important but untranslatable dimension. Since Chinese characters do not vary in length, and because there are exactly five characters per line in a poem like [the one that [Eliot Weinberger](/wiki/Eliot_Weinberger "Eliot Weinberger") discusses in *19 Ways of Looking at [Wang Wei](/wiki/Wang_Wei_(Tang_dynasty) "Wang Wei (Tang dynasty)") (with More Ways)*], another untranslatable feature is that the written result, hung on a wall, presents a rectangle. Translators into languages whose word lengths vary can reproduce such an effect only at the risk of fatal awkwardness....
> Another imponderable is how to imitate the 1-2, 1-2-3 [rhythm](/wiki/Rhythm "Rhythm") in which five-[syllable](/wiki/Syllable "Syllable") lines in classical Chinese poems normally are read. Chinese characters are pronounced in one syllable apiece, so producing such rhythms in Chinese is not hard and the results are unobtrusive; but any imitation in a Western language is almost inevitably stilted and distracting. Even less translatable are the patterns of [tone](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)") arrangement in classical Chinese poetry. Each syllable (character) belongs to one of two categories determined by the [pitch contour](/wiki/Pitch_contour "Pitch contour") in which it is read; in a classical Chinese poem the patterns of alternation of the two categories exhibit [parallelism](/wiki/Parallelism_(grammar) "Parallelism (grammar)") and mirroring.[[23]](#cite_note-LXIII_2016_p._49-23)

Most of the difficulties, according to Link, arise in addressing the second problem, "where the impossibility of perfect answers spawns endless debate." Almost always at the center is the letter-versus-spirit [dilemma](/wiki/Dilemma "Dilemma"). At the literalist extreme, efforts are made to dissect every conceivable detail about the language of the original Chinese poem. "The dissection, though," writes Link, "normally does to the art of a poem approximately what the [scalpel](/wiki/Scalpel "Scalpel") of an [anatomy](/wiki/Anatomy "Anatomy") instructor does to the life of a frog."[[23]](#cite_note-LXIII_2016_p._49-23)

Chinese characters, in avoiding [grammatical](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar") specificity, offer advantages to poets (and, simultaneously, challenges to poetry translators) that are associated primarily with absences of [subject](/wiki/Subject_(grammar) "Subject (grammar)"), [number](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number"), and [tense](/wiki/Grammatical_tense "Grammatical tense").[[24]](#cite_note-LXIII_2016_p._50-24)

It is the norm in classical [Chinese poetry](/wiki/Chinese_poetry "Chinese poetry"), and common even in modern Chinese prose, to omit subjects; the reader or listener infers a subject. The grammars of some Western languages, however, require that a subject be stated (although this is often avoided by using a passive or impersonal construction). Most of the translators cited in Eliot Weinberger's *19 Ways of Looking at [Wang Wei](/wiki/Wang_Wei_(Tang_dynasty) "Wang Wei (Tang dynasty)")* supply a subject. Weinberger points out, however, that when an "I" as a subject is inserted, a "controlling individual mind of the poet" enters and destroys the effect of the Chinese line. Without a subject, he writes, "the experience becomes both universal and immediate to the reader." Another approach to the subjectlessness is to use the target language's [passive voice](/wiki/Passive_voice "Passive voice"); but this again particularizes the experience too much.[[24]](#cite_note-LXIII_2016_p._50-24)

[Nouns](/wiki/Noun "Noun") have no [number](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number") in Chinese. "If," writes Link, "you want to talk in Chinese about one rose, you may, but then you use a "[measure word](/wiki/Measure_word "Measure word")" to say "one blossom-of roseness."[[24]](#cite_note-LXIII_2016_p._50-24)

Chinese [verbs](/wiki/Verb "Verb") are [tense](/wiki/Grammatical_tense "Grammatical tense")-less: there are several ways to specify when something happened or will happen, but [verb tense](/wiki/Verb_tense "Verb tense") is not one of them. For poets, this creates the great advantage of [ambiguity](/wiki/Ambiguity "Ambiguity"). According to Link, Weinberger's insight about subjectlessness—that it produces an effect "both universal and immediate"—applies to timelessness as well.[[24]](#cite_note-LXIII_2016_p._50-24)

Link proposes a kind of uncertainty principle that may be applicable not only to translation from the Chinese language, but to all translation:

> Dilemmas about translation do not have definitive right answers (although there can be unambiguously wrong ones if misreadings of the original are involved). Any translation (except machine translation, a different case) must pass through the mind of a translator, and that mind inevitably contains its own store of perceptions, memories, and values.
> Weinberger [...] pushes this insight further when he writes that "every reading of every poem, regardless of language, is an act of translation: translation into the reader's intellectual and emotional life." Then he goes still further: because a reader's mental life shifts over time, there is a sense in which "the same poem cannot be read twice."[[24]](#cite_note-LXIII_2016_p._50-24)

#### Islamic world

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Islamic world")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Tawqi%27_script_-_Qur%27anic_verses.jpg/250px-Tawqi%27_script_-_Qur%27anic_verses.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tawqi%27_script_-_Qur%27anic_verses.jpg)

[Qur'an](/wiki/Qur%27an "Qur'an") in [tawqi](/wiki/Tawqi "Tawqi"), with [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") translation (smaller words) in [naskh](/wiki/Naskh_(script) "Naskh (script)") (14th century)

Translation of material into [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") expanded after the creation of [Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script") in the 5th century, and gained great importance with the rise of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") and Islamic empires. Arab translation initially focused primarily on politics, rendering Persian, Greek, even Chinese and Indic diplomatic materials into Arabic. It later focused on translating classical Greek and Persian works, as well as some Chinese and Indian texts, into Arabic for scholarly study at major Islamic learning centers, such as the [Al-Karaouine](/wiki/Al-Karaouine "Al-Karaouine") ([Fes](/wiki/Fes "Fes"), [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco")), [Al-Azhar](/wiki/Al-Azhar_Madrasah "Al-Azhar Madrasah") ([Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt")), and the [Nizamiyya of Baghdad](/wiki/Nizamiyya_of_Baghdad "Nizamiyya of Baghdad"). In terms of theory, Arabic translation drew heavily on earlier Near Eastern traditions as well as more contemporary Greek and Persian traditions.

Arabic translation efforts and techniques are important to Western translation traditions due to centuries of close contacts and exchanges. Especially after the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), Europeans began more intensive study of Arabic and Persian translations of classical works as well as scientific and philosophical works of Arab and oriental origins. Arabic, and to a lesser degree Persian, became important sources of material and perhaps of techniques for revitalized Western traditions, which in time would overtake the Islamic and oriental traditions.

In the 19th century, after the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East")'s [Islamic](/wiki/Islam "Islam") clerics and copyists

> had conceded defeat in their centuries-old battle to contain the corrupting effects of the [printing press](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press"), [an] explosion in publishing ... ensued. Along with expanding secular education, printing transformed an overwhelmingly illiterate society into a partly literate one.
>
> In the past, the [sheikhs](/wiki/Sheikh "Sheikh") and the government had exercised a monopoly over knowledge. Now an expanding elite benefitted from a stream of information on virtually anything that interested them. Between 1880 and 1908... more than six hundred newspapers and periodicals were founded in Egypt alone.
>
> The most prominent among them was *al-Muqtataf* ... [It] was the popular expression of a **translation movement** that had begun earlier in the century with military and medical manuals and highlights from the [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") canon. ([Montesquieu](/wiki/Montesquieu "Montesquieu")'s *Considerations on the Romans* and [Fénelon](/wiki/F%C3%A9nelon "Fénelon")'s *Telemachus* had been favorites.)[[25]](#cite_note-debellaigue77-25)

A translator who contributed mightily to the advance of the Islamic Enlightenment was the Egyptian cleric Rifaa al-Tahtawi (1801–73), who had spent five years in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") in the late 1820s, teaching religion to [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") students. After returning to Cairo with the encouragement of [Muhammad Ali](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_of_Egypt "Muhammad Ali of Egypt") (1769–1849), the [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") viceroy of Egypt, al–Tahtawi became head of the new school of languages and embarked on an intellectual revolution by initiating a program to translate some two thousand European and Turkish volumes, ranging from ancient texts on geography and geometry to [Voltaire](/wiki/Voltaire "Voltaire")'s biography of [Peter the Great](/wiki/Peter_the_Great "Peter the Great"), along with the *[Marseillaise](/wiki/Marseillaise "Marseillaise")* and the entire *[Code Napoléon](/wiki/Code_Napol%C3%A9on "Code Napoléon")*. This was the biggest, most meaningful importation of foreign thought into Arabic since [Abbasid](/wiki/Abbasid "Abbasid") times (750–1258).[[26]](#cite_note-26)

> In France al-Tahtawi had been struck by the way the French language... was constantly renewing itself to fit modern ways of living. Yet [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") has its own sources of reinvention. The root system that Arabic shares with other [Semitic](/wiki/Semitic_languages "Semitic languages") tongues such as Hebrew is capable of expanding the meanings of words using structured [consonantal](/wiki/Consonant "Consonant") variations: the word for airplane, for example, has the same root as the word for bird.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Muhammad_Abduh_%28trimmed%29.JPG/250px-Muhammad_Abduh_%28trimmed%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Muhammad_Abduh_(trimmed).JPG)

[Muhammad Abduh](/wiki/Muhammad_Abduh "Muhammad Abduh")

The movement to translate English and European texts transformed the Arabic and [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Turkey "Ottoman Turkey") [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language") languages, and new words, simplified syntax, and directness came to be valued over the previous convolutions. Educated Arabs and Turks in the new professions and the modernized [civil service](/wiki/Civil_service "Civil service") expressed [skepticism](/wiki/Skepticism "Skepticism"), writes [Christopher de Bellaigue](/wiki/Christopher_de_Bellaigue "Christopher de Bellaigue"), "with a freedom that is rarely witnessed today ... No longer was legitimate knowledge defined by texts in the religious schools, interpreted for the most part with stultifying literalness. It had come to include virtually any intellectual production anywhere in the world." One of the [neologisms](/wiki/Neologisms "Neologisms") that, in a way, came to characterize the infusion of new ideas via translation was *"darwiniya"*, or "[Darwinism](/wiki/Darwinism "Darwinism")".[[25]](#cite_note-debellaigue77-25)

One of the most influential liberal Islamic thinkers of the time was [Muhammad Abduh](/wiki/Muhammad_Abduh "Muhammad Abduh") (1849–1905), Egypt's senior judicial authority—its chief [mufti](/wiki/Mufti "Mufti")—at the turn of the 20th century and an admirer of [Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin") who in 1903 visited Darwin's exponent [Herbert Spencer](/wiki/Herbert_Spencer "Herbert Spencer") at his home in [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton "Brighton"). Spencer's view of [society as an organism](/wiki/Social_organism "Social organism") with its own laws of evolution paralleled Abduh's ideas.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

After [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), when Britain and France divided up the Middle East's countries, apart from Turkey, between them, pursuant to the [Sykes-Picot agreement](/wiki/Sykes-Picot_agreement "Sykes-Picot agreement")—in violation of solemn wartime promises of postwar Arab autonomy—there came an immediate reaction: the [Muslim Brotherhood](/wiki/Muslim_Brotherhood "Muslim Brotherhood") emerged in Egypt, the [House of Saud](/wiki/House_of_Saud "House of Saud") took over the [Hijaz](/wiki/Hijaz "Hijaz"), and regimes led by army officers came to power in [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") and Turkey. "[B]oth illiberal currents of the modern Middle East," writes [de Bellaigue](/wiki/Christopher_de_Bellaigue "Christopher de Bellaigue"), "Islamism and militarism, received a major impetus from Western [empire-builders](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism")." As often happens in countries undergoing social crisis, the aspirations of the Muslim world's translators and modernizers, such as Muhammad Abduh, largely had to yield to retrograde currents.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Fidelity and transparency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Fidelity and transparency")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/John_Dryden_by_John_Michael_Wright%2C_1668_%28detail%29%2C_National_Portrait_Gallery%2C_London.JPG/330px-John_Dryden_by_John_Michael_Wright%2C_1668_%28detail%29%2C_National_Portrait_Gallery%2C_London.JPG)](/wiki/File:John_Dryden_by_John_Michael_Wright,_1668_(detail),_National_Portrait_Gallery,_London.JPG)

[Dryden](/wiki/John_Dryden "John Dryden")

[Fidelity](/wiki/Fidelity "Fidelity") (or "faithfulness") and felicity[[30]](#cite_note-This_Little_Art_2018_p._22-30) (or [transparency](/wiki/Transparency_(linguistic) "Transparency (linguistic)")), also known singularly as **equivalence**, are often (though not always) at odds. A 17th-century French critic coined the phrase "*les belles infidèles*" to suggest that translations can be either faithful or beautiful, but not both.[[a]](#cite_note-32) Fidelity is the extent to which a translation accurately renders the [meaning](/wiki/Meaning_(linguistics) "Meaning (linguistics)") of the [source text](/wiki/Source_text "Source text"), without distortion. Transparency is the extent to which a translation appears to a native speaker of the target language to have originally been written in that language, and conforms to its grammar, syntax and idiom. John Dryden (1631–1700) wrote in his preface to the translation anthology *Sylvae*:

> Where I have taken away some of [the original authors'] Expressions, and cut them shorter, it may possibly be on this consideration, that what was beautiful in the Greek or Latin, would not appear so shining in the English; and where I have enlarg'd them, I desire the false Criticks would not always think that those thoughts are wholly mine, but that either they are secretly in the Poet, or may be fairly deduc'd from him; or at least, if both those considerations should fail, that my own is of a piece with his, and that if he were living, and an Englishman, they are such as he wou'd probably have written.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

A translation that meets the criterion of fidelity (faithfulness) is said to be "faithful"; a translation that meets the criterion of transparency, "[idiomatic](/wiki/Idiomatic "Idiomatic")". Depending on the given translation, the two qualities may not be mutually exclusive. The criteria for judging the fidelity of a translation vary according to the subject, type and use of the text, its literary qualities, its social or historical context, etc. The criteria for judging the transparency of a translation appear more straightforward: an unidiomatic translation "sounds wrong" and, in extreme cases of word-for-word translation, often results in patent nonsense.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Friedrich_Daniel_Ernst_Schleiermacher.jpg/330px-Friedrich_Daniel_Ernst_Schleiermacher.jpg)](/wiki/File:Friedrich_Daniel_Ernst_Schleiermacher.jpg)

[Schleiermacher](/wiki/Friedrich_Schleiermacher "Friedrich Schleiermacher")

Nevertheless, in certain contexts a translator may consciously seek to produce a literal translation. Translators of literary, religious, or historic texts often adhere as closely as possible to the source text, stretching the limits of the target language to produce an unidiomatic text. Also, a translator may adopt expressions from the source language in order to provide "local color".

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Lawrence_Venuti.jpeg/250px-Lawrence_Venuti.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Lawrence_Venuti.jpeg)

[Venuti](/wiki/Lawrence_Venuti "Lawrence Venuti")

While current Western translation practice is dominated by the dual concepts of "fidelity" and "transparency", this has not always been the case. There have been periods, especially in pre-Classical Rome and in the 18th century, when many translators stepped beyond the bounds of translation proper into the realm of *[adaptation](/wiki/Adaptation_(arts) "Adaptation (arts)")*. [Adapted translation](/wiki/Adapted_translation "Adapted translation") retains currency in some non-Western traditions. The [Indian](/wiki/India "India") epic, the *[Ramayana](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana")*, appears in many versions in the various [Indian languages](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India"), and the stories are different in each. Similar examples are to be found in [medieval Christian](/wiki/Medieval_Christian "Medieval Christian") literature, which adjusted the text to local customs and mores.

Many non-transparent-translation theories draw on concepts from [German Romanticism](/wiki/German_Romanticism "German Romanticism"), the most obvious influence being the German theologian and philosopher [Friedrich Schleiermacher](/wiki/Friedrich_Schleiermacher "Friedrich Schleiermacher"). In his seminal lecture "On the Different Methods of Translation" (1813) he distinguished between translation methods that move "the writer toward [the reader]", i.e., transparency, and those that move the "reader toward [the author]", i.e., an extreme fidelity to the foreignness of the source text. Schleiermacher favored the latter approach; he was motivated, however, not so much by a desire to embrace the foreign, as by a nationalist desire to oppose France's cultural domination and to promote [German literature](/wiki/German_literature "German literature")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

In recent decades, prominent advocates of such "non-transparent" translation have included the French scholar [Antoine Berman](/wiki/Antoine_Berman "Antoine Berman"), who identified twelve deforming tendencies inherent in most prose translations,[[33]](#cite_note-34) and the American theorist [Lawrence Venuti](/wiki/Lawrence_Venuti "Lawrence Venuti"), who has called on translators to apply "foreignizing" rather than domesticating translation strategies.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

### Equivalence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Equivalence")]

Main article: [Semantic equivalence (translation)](/wiki/Semantic_equivalence_(translation) "Semantic equivalence (translation)")

The question of [fidelity](/wiki/Fidelity "Fidelity") vs. [transparency](/wiki/Transparency_(linguistic) "Transparency (linguistic)") has also been formulated in terms of, respectively, "*formal* equivalence" and "*dynamic* [or *functional*] equivalence" – expressions associated with the translator [Eugene Nida](/wiki/Eugene_Nida "Eugene Nida") and originally coined to describe ways of translating the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"); but the two approaches are applicable to any translation. "Formal equivalence" corresponds to "metaphrase", and "dynamic equivalence" to "paraphrase". "Formal equivalence" (sought via "literal" translation) attempts to render the text literally, or "word for word" (the latter expression being itself a word-for-word rendering of the [classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin") *verbum pro verbo*) – if necessary, at the expense of features natural to the target language. By contrast, "dynamic equivalence" (or "*functional* equivalence") conveys the essential thoughts expressed in a source text—if necessary, at the expense of literality, original [sememe](/wiki/Sememe "Sememe") and [word order](/wiki/Word_order "Word order"), the source text's active vs. passive [voice](/wiki/Voice_(grammar) "Voice (grammar)"), etc.

There is, however, no sharp boundary between formal and functional equivalence. On the contrary, they represent a spectrum of translation approaches. Each is used at various times and in various contexts by the same translator, and at various points within the same text – sometimes simultaneously. Competent translation entails the judicious blending of formal and functional [equivalents](/wiki/Dynamic_and_formal_equivalence "Dynamic and formal equivalence").[[35]](#cite_note-36)

Common pitfalls in translation, especially when practiced by inexperienced translators, involve false equivalents such as "[false friends](/wiki/False_friend "False friend")"[[36]](#cite_note-37) and [false cognates](/wiki/False_cognate "False cognate").

### Source and target languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Source and target languages")]

In the practice of translation, the **source language** is the language being translated from, while the **target language** – also called the **receptor language**[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39) – is the language being translated into.[[39]](#cite_note-40) Difficulties in translating can arise from [lexical](/wiki/Lexicon "Lexicon") and [syntactical](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax") differences between the source language and the target language, which differences tend to be greater between two languages belonging to different [language families](/wiki/Language_families "Language families").[[40]](#cite_note-41)

Often the source language is the translator's [second language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language"), while the target language is the translator's [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language").[[41]](#cite_note-42) In some geographical settings, however, the source language is the translator's first language because not enough people speak the source language as a second language.[[42]](#cite_note-Pokorn2007-43) For instance, a 2005 survey found that 89% of professional Slovene translators translate into their second language, usually English.[[42]](#cite_note-Pokorn2007-43) In cases where the source language is the translator's first language, the translation process has been referred to by various terms, including "translating into a non-mother tongue", "translating into a second language", "inverse translation", "reverse translation", "service translation", and "translation from A to B".[[42]](#cite_note-Pokorn2007-43) The process typically begins with a full and in-depth analysis of the original text in the source language, ensuring full comprehension and understanding before the actual act of translating is approached.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

Translation for specialized or professional fields requires a working knowledge, as well, of the pertinent terminology in the field. For example, translation of a legal text requires not only fluency in the respective languages but also familiarity with the terminology specific to the legal field in each language.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

While the form and style of the source language often cannot be reproduced in the target language, the meaning and content can. Linguist [Roman Jakobson](/wiki/Roman_Jakobson "Roman Jakobson") went so far as to assert that all cognitive experience can be classified and expressed in any living language.[[45]](#cite_note-46) Linguist [Ghil'ad Zuckermann](/wiki/Ghil%27ad_Zuckermann "Ghil'ad Zuckermann") suggests that the limits are not of translation *per se* but rather of *elegant* translation.[[46]](#cite_note-Revivalistics-47): 219

#### Source and target texts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Source and target texts")]

See also: [Source text](/wiki/Source_text "Source text")

In translation, a **[source text](/wiki/Source_text "Source text")** (**ST**) is a text written in a given source language which is to be, or has been, translated into another language, while a **target text** (**TT**) is a translated text written in the intended target language, which is the result of a translation from a given source text. According to [Jeremy Munday](/wiki/Jeremy_Munday "Jeremy Munday")'s definition of translation, "the process of translation between two different written languages involves the changing of an original written text (the source text or ST) in the original verbal language (the source language or SL) into a written text (the target text or TT) in a different verbal language (the target language or TL)".[[47]](#cite_note-48) The terms 'source text' and 'target text' are preferred over 'original' and 'translation' because they do not have the same positive vs. negative value judgment.

Translation scholars including [Eugene Nida](/wiki/Eugene_Nida "Eugene Nida") and [Peter Newmark](/wiki/Peter_Newmark "Peter Newmark") have represented the different approaches to translation as falling broadly into source-text-oriented or target-text-oriented categories.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

#### Back-translation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Back-translation")]

A "back-translation" is a translation of a translated text back into the language of the original text, made without reference to the original text. Comparison of a back-translation with the original text is sometimes used as a check on the accuracy of the original translation, much as the accuracy of a mathematical operation is sometimes checked by reversing the operation.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

But the results of such reverse-translation operations, while useful as approximate checks, are not always precisely reliable.[[50]](#cite_note-51) Back-translation must in general be less accurate than back-calculation because [linguistic](/wiki/Linguistic "Linguistic") symbols ([words](/wiki/Word "Word")) are often [ambiguous](/wiki/Ambiguous "Ambiguous"), whereas mathematical symbols are intentionally unequivocal.

In the context of machine translation, a back-translation is also called a "round-trip translation." When translations are produced of material used in medical [clinical trials](/wiki/Clinical_trial "Clinical trial"), such as [informed-consent forms](/wiki/Informed_consent "Informed consent"), a back-translation is often required by the [ethics committee](/wiki/Ethics_Committee_(European_Union) "Ethics Committee (European Union)") or [institutional review board](/wiki/Institutional_review_board "Institutional review board").[[51]](#cite_note-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Mark_Twain%2C_Brady-Handy_photo_portrait%2C_Feb_7%2C_1871%2C_cropped.jpg/250px-Mark_Twain%2C_Brady-Handy_photo_portrait%2C_Feb_7%2C_1871%2C_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mark_Twain,_Brady-Handy_photo_portrait,_Feb_7,_1871,_cropped.jpg)

In 1903, [Mark Twain](/wiki/Mark_Twain "Mark Twain") back-translated his own [short story](/wiki/Short_story "Short story"), "[The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County](/wiki/The_Celebrated_Jumping_Frog_of_Calaveras_County "The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County")".

[Mark Twain](/wiki/Mark_Twain "Mark Twain") provided humorously telling evidence for the frequent unreliability of back-translation when he issued his own back-translation of a French translation of his [short story](/wiki/Short_story "Short story"), "[The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County](/wiki/The_Celebrated_Jumping_Frog_of_Calaveras_County "The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County")". He published his back-translation in a 1903 volume together with his English-language original, the French translation, and a "Private History of the 'Jumping Frog' Story". The latter volume included a synopsized adaptation of his story that Twain stated had appeared, unattributed to Twain, in a Professor [Sidgwick](/wiki/Arthur_Sidgwick "Arthur Sidgwick")'s *Greek Prose Composition* (p. 116) under the title, "The Athenian and the Frog"; the adaptation had for a time been taken for an independent [ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") precursor to Twain's "Jumping Frog" story.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

When a document survives only in translation, the original having been lost, researchers sometimes undertake back-translation in an effort to reconstruct the original text. An example involves the novel *[The Saragossa Manuscript](/wiki/The_Saragossa_Manuscript "The Saragossa Manuscript")* by the Polish aristocrat [Jan Potocki](/wiki/Jan_Potocki "Jan Potocki") (1761–1815), who wrote the novel in French and anonymously published fragments in 1804 and 1813–14. Portions of the original French-language manuscript were subsequently lost; however, the missing fragments survived in a Polish translation, made by [Edmund Chojecki](/wiki/Edmund_Chojecki "Edmund Chojecki") in 1847 from a complete French copy that has since been lost. French-language versions of the complete *Saragossa Manuscript* have since been produced, based on extant French-language fragments and on French-language versions that have been back-translated from Chojecki's Polish version.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

Many works by the influential [Classical](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") physician [Galen](/wiki/Galen "Galen") survive only in medieval [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") translation. Some survive only in [Renaissance Latin](/wiki/Renaissance_Latin "Renaissance Latin") translations from the Arabic, thus at a second remove from the original. To better understand Galen, scholars have attempted back-translation of such works in order to reconstruct the original [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek").[[54]](#cite_note-55)

When historians suspect that a document is actually a translation from another language, back-translation into that hypothetical original language can provide supporting evidence by showing that such characteristics as [idioms](/wiki/Idiom "Idiom"), [puns](/wiki/Pun "Pun"), peculiar [grammatical](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar") structures, etc., are in fact derived from the original language. For example, the known text of the *[Till Eulenspiegel](/wiki/Till_Eulenspiegel "Till Eulenspiegel")* folk tales is in [High German](/wiki/High_German "High German") but contains puns that work only when back-translated to [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German"). This seems clear evidence that these tales (or at least large portions of them) were originally written in Low German and translated into High German by an over-[metaphrastic](/wiki/Metaphrase "Metaphrase") translator.

Supporters of [Aramaic primacy](/wiki/Aramaic_primacy "Aramaic primacy")—the view that the [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") or its sources were originally written in the [Aramaic language](/wiki/Aramaic_language "Aramaic language")—seek to prove their case by showing that difficult passages in the existing [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") text of the New Testament make much more sense when back-translated to Aramaic: that, for example, some incomprehensible references are in fact Aramaic puns that do not work in Greek. Due to similar indications, it is believed that the 2nd-century Gnostic [Gospel of Judas](/wiki/Gospel_of_Judas "Gospel of Judas"), which survives only in [Coptic](/wiki/Coptic_language "Coptic language"), was originally written in Greek.

[John Dryden](/wiki/John_Dryden "John Dryden") (1631–1700), the dominant English-language literary figure of his age, illustrates, in his use of back-translation, translators' influence on the evolution of languages and literary styles. Dryden is believed to be the first person to posit that English sentences should not end in [prepositions](/wiki/Preposition "Preposition") because Latin sentences cannot end in prepositions.[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57) Dryden created the proscription against "[preposition stranding](/wiki/Preposition_stranding "Preposition stranding")" in 1672 when he objected to [Ben Jonson](/wiki/Ben_Jonson "Ben Jonson")'s 1611 phrase, "the bodies that those souls were frighted from", though he did not provide the rationale for his preference.[[57]](#cite_note-58) Dryden often translated his writing into Latin, to check whether his writing was concise and elegant, Latin being considered an elegant and long-lived language with which to compare; then he back-translated his writing back to English according to Latin-grammar usage. As Latin does not have sentences ending in prepositions, Dryden may have applied Latin grammar to English, thus forming the controversial rule of [no sentence-ending prepositions](/wiki/Preposition_stranding#The_Debate_about_P-stranding "Preposition stranding"), subsequently adopted by other writers.[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[b]](#cite_note-60)

## Translators

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Translators")]

A [language](/wiki/Language "Language") is not merely a collection of [words](/wiki/Word "Word") and of rules of [grammar](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar") and [syntax](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax") for generating [sentences](/wiki/Sentence_(linguistics) "Sentence (linguistics)"), but also a vast interconnecting system of [connotations](/wiki/Connotation "Connotation") and [cultural](/wiki/Cultural "Cultural") references whose mastery, writes [linguist](/wiki/Linguist "Linguist") [Mario Pei](/wiki/Mario_Pei "Mario Pei"), "comes close to being a lifetime job."[[59]](#cite_note-61)

[Michael Wood](/wiki/Michael_Wood_(literary_scholar) "Michael Wood (literary scholar)"), a [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") emeritus professor, writes: "[T]ranslation, like language itself, involves contexts, conventions, class, irony, posture and many other regions where [speech acts](/wiki/Speech_act "Speech act") hang out. This is why it helps to compare translations [of a given work]."[[60]](#cite_note-62)

[Emily Wilson](/wiki/Emily_Wilson_(classicist) "Emily Wilson (classicist)"), a professor of classical studies at the [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania") and herself a translator, writes:

> [I]t is [hard] to produce a good literary translation. This is certainly true of translations of [ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") and [Roman](/wiki/Latin "Latin") texts, but it is also true of literary translation in general: it is very difficult. Most readers of foreign languages are not translators; most writers are not translators. Translators have to read and write at the same time, as if always playing multiple instruments in a [one-person band](/wiki/One-person_band "One-person band"). And most one-person bands do not sound very good.[[61]](#cite_note-63)

When in 1921, three years before his death, the English-language novelist [Joseph Conrad](/wiki/Joseph_Conrad "Joseph Conrad") – who had long had little contact with everyday spoken Polish – attempted to translate into English [Bruno Winawer](/wiki/Bruno_Winawer "Bruno Winawer")'s short Polish-language play, *The Book of Job*, he predictably missed many crucial nuances of contemporary Polish language.[[62]](#cite_note-64)

The translator's role, in relation to the original text, has been compared to the roles of other interpretive artists, e.g., a musician or actor who interprets a work of musical or dramatic art. Translating, especially a text of any complexity (like other human activities[[63]](#cite_note-65)), involves *interpretation*: choices must be made, which implies interpretation.[[12]](#cite_note-Kasparek_p._85-12)[[c]](#cite_note-66)[[d]](#cite_note-68) Mark Polizzotti writes: "A good translation offers not a reproduction of the work but an interpretation, a re-representation, just as the performance of a [play](/wiki/Play_(theatre) "Play (theatre)") or a [sonata](/wiki/Sonata "Sonata") is a representation of the [script](/wiki/Play_(theatre) "Play (theatre)") or the [score](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music"), one among many possible representations."[[65]](#cite_note-69) A translation of a text of any complexity is – as, itself, a work of art – unique and unrepeatable.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Joseph_Conrad-remastered_to_black_and_white.png/250px-Joseph_Conrad-remastered_to_black_and_white.png)](/wiki/File:Joseph_Conrad-remastered_to_black_and_white.png)

[Joseph Conrad](/wiki/Joseph_Conrad "Joseph Conrad")

Joseph Conrad, whose writings [Zdzisław Najder](/wiki/Zdzis%C5%82aw_Najder "Zdzisław Najder") has described as verging on "auto-translation" from Conrad's Polish and French linguistic personae,[[66]](#cite_note-70) advised his niece and [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") translator [Aniela Zagórska](/wiki/Aniela_Zag%C3%B3rska "Aniela Zagórska"):

> [D]on't trouble to be too scrupulous ... I may tell you (in French) that in my opinion *il vaut mieux interpréter que traduire* [it is better to interpret than to translate] ...*Il s'agit donc de trouver les équivalents. Et là, ma chère, je vous prie laissez vous guider plutôt par votre tempérament que par une conscience sévère ...* [It is, then, a question of finding the equivalent expressions. And there, my dear, I beg you to let yourself be guided more by your temperament than by a strict conscience....]"[[67]](#cite_note-71)

Conrad advised another translator that the prime requisite for a good translation is that it be "idiomatic". "For in the [idiom](/wiki/Idiom "Idiom") is the *clearness* of a language and the language's force and its picturesqueness—by which last I mean the picture-producing power of arranged words."[[68]](#cite_note-72) Conrad thought [C.K. Scott Moncrieff](/wiki/C.K._Scott_Moncrieff "C.K. Scott Moncrieff")'s English translation of [Marcel Proust](/wiki/Marcel_Proust "Marcel Proust")'s *À la recherche du temps perdu* (*[In Search of Lost Time](/wiki/In_Search_of_Lost_Time "In Search of Lost Time")*—or, in Scott Moncrieff's rendering, *Remembrance of Things Past*) to be preferable to the French original.[[69]](#cite_note-73)[[e]](#cite_note-74)

Emily Wilson writes that "translation always involves interpretation, and [requires] every translator... to think as deeply as humanly possible about each verbal, poetic, and interpretative [choice](/wiki/Choice "Choice")."[[70]](#cite_note-75)

[Daniel Mendelsohn](/wiki/Daniel_Mendelsohn "Daniel Mendelsohn"), a [classicist](/wiki/Classics "Classics") at [Bard College](/wiki/Bard_College "Bard College"), has similarly said, in an interview, that

> a lot of people, when they think about translation, think you sit down with a dictionary and look at the text and get going, but you really have to have an interpretation.... You need to make a decision. I don’t think there’s any translation that is not also an interpretation. There’s no such thing as an absolutely transparent translation.[[71]](#cite_note-76)

Translation of other than the simplest brief texts requires painstakingly [close reading](/wiki/Close_reading "Close reading") of the [source text](/wiki/Source_text "Source text") and the draft translation, so as to resolve the ambiguities inherent in [language](/wiki/Language "Language") and thereby to [asymptotically](/wiki/Asymptotically "Asymptotically") approach the most accurate rendering of the source text.[[72]](#cite_note-Pharaoh_2020-77)

Part of the ambiguity, for a translator, involves the structure of human language. [Psychologist](/wiki/Psychologist "Psychologist") and [neural scientist](/wiki/Neural_science "Neural science") [Gary Marcus](/wiki/Gary_Marcus "Gary Marcus") notes that "virtually every sentence [that people generate] is [ambiguous](/wiki/Ambiguous "Ambiguous"), often in multiple ways. Our brain is so good at comprehending language that we do not usually notice."[[73]](#cite_note-78) An example of linguistic ambiguity is the "pronoun disambiguation problem" ("PDP"): a machine has no way of determining to whom or what a [pronoun](/wiki/Pronoun "Pronoun") in a sentence—such as "he", "she" or "it"—refers.[[74]](#cite_note-79) Such disambiguation is not infallible by a human, either.

Ambiguity is a concern both to translators and – as the writings of poet and literary critic [William Empson](/wiki/William_Empson "William Empson") have demonstrated – to [literary critics](/wiki/Literary_critics "Literary critics"). Ambiguity may be desirable, indeed essential, in [poetry](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry") and [diplomacy](/wiki/Diplomacy "Diplomacy"); it can be more problematic in ordinary [prose](/wiki/Prose "Prose").[[75]](#cite_note-80)

Individual [expressions](/wiki/Expression_(linguistics) "Expression (linguistics)") – [words](/wiki/Word "Word"), [phrases](/wiki/Phrase "Phrase"), [sentences](/wiki/Sentence_(linguistics) "Sentence (linguistics)") – are fraught with [connotations](/wiki/Connotation "Connotation"). As Empson demonstrates, any piece of language seems susceptible to "alternative reactions", or as Joseph Conrad once wrote, "No English word has clean edges." All expressions, Conrad thought, carried so many connotations as to be little more than "instruments for exciting blurred emotions."[[76]](#cite_note-81)

Translators may render only parts of the original text, provided that they inform readers of that action. But a translator should not assume the role of [censor](/wiki/Censorship "Censorship") and surreptitiously delete or [bowdlerize](/wiki/Bowdlerize "Bowdlerize") passages merely to please a political or moral interest.[[77]](#cite_note-Billiani,_Francesca_2001-82)

Translating has served as a school of writing for many an author, much as the copying of masterworks of [painting](/wiki/Painting "Painting") has schooled many a novice painter.[[78]](#cite_note-83) A translator who can competently render an author's thoughts into the translator's own language, should certainly be able to adequately render, in his own language, any thoughts of his own. Translating (like [analytic philosophy](/wiki/Analytic_philosophy "Analytic philosophy")) compels precise analysis of [language elements](/wiki/Language "Language") and of their usage. In 1946 the poet [Ezra Pound](/wiki/Ezra_Pound "Ezra Pound"), then at [St. Elizabeth's Hospital](/wiki/St._Elizabeth%27s_Hospital "St. Elizabeth's Hospital"), in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), advised a visitor, the 18-year-old beginning poet [W.S. Merwin](/wiki/W.S._Merwin "W.S. Merwin"): "The work of translation is the best teacher you'll ever have."[[79]](#cite_note-84)[[f]](#cite_note-86) Merwin, translator-poet who took Pound's advice to heart, writes of translation as an "impossible, unfinishable" art.[[81]](#cite_note-87)

A translator acts as a bridge between two languages and cultures. When he has completed the first draft of a translation, he stands at the bridge's midpoint. Only after he has fully converted the vocabulary, idioms, grammar, and syntax of the source text to those of the target language, does he arrive at the bridge's other end.

Translators, including monks who spread [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") texts in [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"), and the early modern European translators of the Bible, in the course of their work have shaped the very languages into which they have translated. They have acted as bridges for conveying knowledge between [cultures](/wiki/Culture "Culture"); and along with ideas, they have imported from the source languages, into their own languages, loanwords and calques of [grammatical structures](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar"), [idioms](/wiki/Idiom "Idiom"), and [vocabulary](/wiki/Vocabulary "Vocabulary").

### Interpreting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Interpreting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Cortez_%26_La_Malinche.jpg/330px-Cortez_%26_La_Malinche.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cortez_%26_La_Malinche.jpg)

[Hernán Cortés](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Cort%C3%A9s "Hernán Cortés") and [La Malinche](/wiki/La_Malinche "La Malinche") (*right*) meet [Moctezuma II](/wiki/Moctezuma_II "Moctezuma II") in [Tenochtitlan](/wiki/Tenochtitlan "Tenochtitlan"), 8 November 1519.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Lewis_and_Clark_1954_Issue-3c.jpg/330px-Lewis_and_Clark_1954_Issue-3c.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lewis_and_Clark_1954_Issue-3c.jpg)

[Lewis and Clark](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition") and their [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") interpreter, [Sacagawea](/wiki/Sacagawea "Sacagawea")

Main article: [Interpreting](/wiki/Interpreting "Interpreting")

[Interpreting](/wiki/Interpreting "Interpreting") is the facilitation of [oral](/wiki/Speech_communication "Speech communication") or [sign-language](/wiki/Sign-language "Sign-language") [communication](/wiki/Communication "Communication"), either simultaneously or consecutively, between two, or among three or more, speakers who are not speaking, or signing, the same language. The term "interpreting," rather than "interpretation," is preferentially used for this activity by Anglophone interpreters and translators, to avoid confusion with other meanings of the word "[interpretation](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/interpret "wikt:interpret")."

Unlike English, many languages do not employ two separate words to denote the activities of [written](/wiki/Written "Written") and live-communication ([oral](/wiki/Speech_communication "Speech communication") or [sign-language](/wiki/Sign-language "Sign-language")) translators.[[g]](#cite_note-89) Even English does not always make the distinction, frequently using "translating" as a synonym for "interpreting."

Interpreters have sometimes played crucial roles in [human history](/wiki/Human_history "Human history"). A prime example is [La Malinche](/wiki/La_Malinche "La Malinche"), also known as *Malintzin*, *Malinalli* and *Doña Marina*, an early-16th-century [Nahua](/wiki/Nahua "Nahua") woman from the Mexican [Gulf Coast](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"). As a child she had been sold or given to [Maya](/wiki/Maya_peoples "Maya peoples") slave-traders from Xicalango, and thus had become bilingual. Subsequently, given along with other women to the invading Spaniards, she became instrumental in the [Spanish](/wiki/Spain "Spain") conquest of [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), acting as interpreter, adviser, intermediary and lover to [Hernán Cortés](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Cort%C3%A9s "Hernán Cortés").[[83]](#cite_note-90)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Lin_Shu.jpg/250px-Lin_Shu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lin_Shu.jpg)

[Lin Shu](/wiki/Lin_Shu "Lin Shu")

Nearly three centuries later, in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), a comparable role as interpreter was played for the [Lewis and Clark Expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition") of 1804–6 by [Sacagawea](/wiki/Sacagawea "Sacagawea"). As a child, the [Lemhi Shoshone](/wiki/Lemhi_Shoshone "Lemhi Shoshone") woman had been kidnapped by [Hidatsa](/wiki/Hidatsa "Hidatsa") Indians and thus had become bilingual. Sacagawea facilitated the expedition's traverse of the [North American continent](/wiki/North_American_continent "North American continent") to the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean").[[84]](#cite_note-91)

The famous Chinese man of letters [Lin Shu](/wiki/Lin_Shu "Lin Shu") (1852 – 1924), who knew no foreign languages, rendered Western literary classics into Chinese with the help of his friend Wang Shouchang (王壽昌), who had studied in France. Wang interpreted the texts for Lin, who rendered them into Chinese. Lin's first such translation, 巴黎茶花女遺事 (*Past Stories of the Camellia-woman of Paris* – [Alexandre Dumas, fils](/wiki/Alexandre_Dumas,_fils "Alexandre Dumas, fils")'s, *[La Dame aux Camélias](/wiki/La_Dame_aux_Cam%C3%A9lias "La Dame aux Camélias")*), published in 1899, was an immediate success and was followed by many more translations from the French and the English.[[85]](#cite_note-92)

### Sworn translation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sworn translation")]

[Sworn translation](/wiki/Sworn_translation "Sworn translation"), also called "certified translation," aims at legal equivalence between two documents written in different languages. It is performed by someone authorized to do so by local regulations, which vary widely from country to country. Some countries recognize self-declared competence. Others require the translator to be an official state appointee. In some countries, such as the United Kingdom, certain government institutions require that translators be accredited by certain translation institutes or associations in order to be able to carry out certified translations.

### Internet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Internet")]

Web-based human translation is generally favored by companies and individuals that wish to secure more accurate translations. In view of the frequent inaccuracy of machine translations, human translation remains the most reliable, most accurate form of translation available.[[86]](#cite_note-93) With the recent emergence of translation [crowdsourcing](/wiki/Crowdsourcing "Crowdsourcing"),[[87]](#cite_note-94) [translation memory](/wiki/Translation_memory "Translation memory") techniques, and [internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") applications,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] translation agencies have been able to provide on-demand human-translation services to [businesses](/wiki/Small_and_medium_businesses "Small and medium businesses"), individuals, and enterprises.

While not instantaneous like its machine counterparts such as [Google Translate](/wiki/Google_Translate "Google Translate") and [Babel Fish](/wiki/Babel_Fish_(website) "Babel Fish (website)") (now defunct), as of 2010 web-based human translation has been gaining popularity by providing relatively fast, accurate translation of business communications, legal documents, medical records, and [software localization](/wiki/Software_localization "Software localization").[[88]](#cite_note-95) Web-based human translation also appeals to private website users and bloggers.[[89]](#cite_note-96) Contents of websites are translatable but URLs of websites are not translatable into other languages. Language tools on the internet provide help in understanding text.

### Computer assist

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Computer assist")]

Main article: [Computer-assisted translation](/wiki/Computer-assisted_translation "Computer-assisted translation")

Computer-assisted translation (CAT), also called "computer-aided translation," "machine-aided human translation" (MAHT) and "interactive translation," is a form of translation wherein a human translator creates a [target text](#Source_and_target_texts) with the assistance of a computer program. The machine supports a human translator.

Computer-assisted translation can include standard [dictionary](/wiki/Dictionary "Dictionary") and grammar software. The term, however, normally refers to a range of specialized programs available to the translator, including translation memory, [terminology](/wiki/Terminology "Terminology")-management, [concordance](/wiki/Concordancer "Concordancer"), and alignment programs.

These tools speed up and facilitate human translation, but they do not provide translation. The latter is a function of tools known broadly as machine translation. The tools speed up the translation process by assisting the human translator by memorizing or committing translations to a database (translation memory database) so that if the same sentence occurs in the same project or a future project, the content can be reused. This translation reuse leads to cost savings, better consistency and shorter project timelines.

## Machine translation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Machine translation")]

Main article: [Machine translation](/wiki/Machine_translation "Machine translation")

Machine translation (MT) is a process whereby a computer program analyzes a [source text](/wiki/Source_text "Source text") and, in principle, produces a target text without human intervention. In reality, however, machine translation typically does involve human intervention, in the form of pre-editing and [post-editing](/wiki/Post-editing "Post-editing").[[90]](#cite_note-NIST-97) With proper [terminology](/wiki/Terminology "Terminology") work, with preparation of the [source text](/wiki/Source_text "Source text") for machine translation (pre-editing), and with reworking of the machine translation by a human translator (post-editing), commercial machine-translation tools can produce useful results, especially if the machine-translation system is integrated with a translation memory or [translation management system](/wiki/Translation_management_system "Translation management system").[[91]](#cite_note-98)

Unedited machine translation is publicly available through tools on the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") such as [Google Translate](/wiki/Google_Translate "Google Translate"), [Almaany](/wiki/Almaany "Almaany"),[[92]](#cite_note-Altarabin2020-99) [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon_Software "Babylon Software"), [DeepL Translator](/wiki/DeepL_Translator "DeepL Translator"), and [StarDict](/wiki/StarDict "StarDict"). These produce rough translations that, under favorable circumstances, approximate the meaning of the source text. With the Internet, translation software can help non-native-speaking individuals understand web pages published in other languages. Whole-page-translation tools are of limited utility, however, since they offer only a limited potential understanding of the original author's intent and context; translated pages tend to be more erroneously humorous and confusing than enlightening.

Interactive translations with [pop-up windows](/wiki/Pop-up_ad "Pop-up ad") are becoming more popular. These tools show one or more possible equivalents for each word or phrase. Human operators merely need to select the likeliest equivalent as the mouse glides over the foreign-language text. Possible equivalents can be grouped by pronunciation. Also, companies such as [Ectaco](/wiki/Ectaco "Ectaco") produce pocket devices that provide machine translations.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/1b/Claude_Piron.jpg)](/wiki/File:Claude_Piron.jpg)

[Claude Piron](/wiki/Claude_Piron "Claude Piron")

Relying exclusively on unedited machine translation, however, ignores the fact that communication in [human language](/wiki/Natural_language "Natural language") is [context](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/context "wikt:context")-embedded and that it takes a person to comprehend the context of the original text with a reasonable degree of probability. It is certainly true that even purely human-generated translations are prone to error; therefore, to ensure that a machine-generated translation will be useful to a human being and that publishable-quality translation is achieved, such translations must be reviewed and edited by a human.[[h]](#cite_note-101) [Claude Piron](/wiki/Claude_Piron "Claude Piron") writes that machine translation, at its best, automates the easier part of a translator's job; the harder and more time-consuming part usually involves doing extensive research to resolve [ambiguities](/wiki/Ambiguities "Ambiguities") in the [source text](/wiki/Source_text "Source text"), which the [grammatical](/wiki/Grammatical "Grammatical") and [lexical](/wiki/Lexical_(semiotics) "Lexical (semiotics)") exigencies of the target language require to be resolved.[[94]](#cite_note-102) Such research is a necessary prelude to the pre-editing necessary in order to provide input for machine-translation software, such that the output will not be [meaningless](/wiki/Garbage_in_garbage_out "Garbage in garbage out").[[90]](#cite_note-NIST-97)

The weaknesses of pure machine translation, unaided by human expertise, are those of artificial intelligence itself.[[95]](#cite_note-103) As of 2018, professional translator Mark Polizzotti held that machine translation, by [Google Translate](/wiki/Google_Translate "Google Translate") and the like, was unlikely to threaten human translators anytime soon, because machines would never grasp nuance and [connotation](/wiki/Connotation "Connotation").[[96]](#cite_note-104) Writes Paul Taylor: "Perhaps there is a limit to what a computer can do without knowing that it is manipulating imperfect representations of an external reality."[[97]](#cite_note-105)

[Gary Marcus](/wiki/Gary_Marcus "Gary Marcus") notes that a so far insuperable stumbling block to artificial intelligence is an incapacity for reliable [disambiguation](/wiki/Disambiguation "Disambiguation"). "[V]irtually every sentence [that people generate] is [ambiguous](/wiki/Ambiguous "Ambiguous"), often in multiple ways." A prominent example is known as the "pronoun disambiguation problem": a machine has no way of determining to whom or what a [pronoun](/wiki/Pronoun "Pronoun") in a sentence—such as "he", "she" or "it"—refers.[[98]](#cite_note-106)

## Literary translation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Literary translation")]

Translation of [literary works](/wiki/Literary_works "Literary works") ([novels](/wiki/Novel "Novel"), [short stories](/wiki/Short_stories "Short stories"), [plays](/wiki/Theatre "Theatre"), [poems](/wiki/Poems "Poems"), etc.) is considered a literary pursuit in its own right. Notable in [Canadian literature](/wiki/Canadian_literature "Canadian literature") *specifically* as translators are figures such as [Sheila Fischman](/wiki/Sheila_Fischman "Sheila Fischman"), [Robert Dickson](/wiki/Robert_Dickson_(writer) "Robert Dickson (writer)"), and [Linda Gaboriau](/wiki/Linda_Gaboriau "Linda Gaboriau"); and the Canadian [Governor General's Awards](/wiki/Governor_General%27s_Awards "Governor General's Awards") annually present prizes for the best English-to-French and French-to-English literary translations.

Other writers, among many who have made a name for themselves as literary translators, include [Vasily Zhukovsky](/wiki/Vasily_Zhukovsky "Vasily Zhukovsky"), [Tadeusz Boy-Żeleński](/wiki/Tadeusz_Boy-%C5%BBele%C5%84ski "Tadeusz Boy-Żeleński"), [Vladimir Nabokov](/wiki/Vladimir_Nabokov "Vladimir Nabokov"), [Jorge Luis Borges](/wiki/Jorge_Luis_Borges "Jorge Luis Borges"), [Robert Stiller](/wiki/Robert_Stiller "Robert Stiller"), [Lydia Davis](/wiki/Lydia_Davis "Lydia Davis"), [Haruki Murakami](/wiki/Haruki_Murakami "Haruki Murakami"), [Achy Obejas](/wiki/Achy_Obejas "Achy Obejas"), and [Jhumpa Lahiri](/wiki/Jhumpa_Lahiri "Jhumpa Lahiri").
