In the 2010s a substantial gender imbalance was noted in literary translation into English,[[99]](#cite_note-107) with far more male writers being translated than women writers. In 2014 Meytal Radzinski launched the *Women in Translation* campaign to address this.[[100]](#cite_note-108)[[101]](#cite_note-109)[[102]](#cite_note-110)

### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: History")]

The first important translation in the West was that of the [Septuagint](/wiki/Septuagint "Septuagint"), a collection of [Jewish](/wiki/Jew "Jew") Scriptures translated into early [Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek") in [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") between the 3rd and 1st centuries BCE. The dispersed Jews had forgotten their ancestral language and needed Greek versions (translations) of their Scriptures.[[103]](#cite_note-111)

Throughout the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), Latin was the *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* of the western learned world. The 9th-century [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great"), king of [Wessex](/wiki/Wessex "Wessex") in [England](/wiki/England "England"), was far ahead of his time in commissioning [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_language "Anglo-Saxon language") translations of [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede")'s *[Ecclesiastical History](/wiki/Historia_ecclesiastica_gentis_Anglorum "Historia ecclesiastica gentis Anglorum")* and [Boethius](/wiki/Boethius "Boethius")' *[Consolation of Philosophy](/wiki/Consolation_of_Philosophy "Consolation of Philosophy")*. Meanwhile, the [Christian Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church") frowned on even partial adaptations of [St. Jerome](/wiki/St._Jerome "St. Jerome")'s [Vulgate](/wiki/Vulgate "Vulgate") of c. 384 CE,[[104]](#cite_note-112) the standard Latin Bible.

In [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), the spread of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") led to large-scale ongoing translation efforts spanning well over a thousand years. The [Tangut Empire](/wiki/Tangut_Empire "Tangut Empire") was especially efficient in such efforts; exploiting the then newly invented [block printing](/wiki/Block_printing "Block printing"), and with the full support of the government (contemporary sources describe the Emperor and his mother personally contributing to the translation effort, alongside sages of various nationalities), the Tanguts took mere decades to translate volumes that had taken the [Chinese](/wiki/China "China") centuries to render.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs") undertook [large-scale efforts at translation](/wiki/Graeco-Arabic_translation_movement "Graeco-Arabic translation movement"). Having conquered the [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") world, they made Arabic versions of its philosophical and scientific works. During the Middle Ages, translations of some of these Arabic versions [were made into Latin](/wiki/Latin_translations_of_the_12th_century "Latin translations of the 12th century"), chiefly at [Córdoba](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Spain "Córdoba, Spain") in [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain").[[105]](#cite_note-Cohen13-113) King [Alfonso X the Wise](/wiki/Alfonso_X_the_Wise "Alfonso X the Wise") of [Castile](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Castile "Kingdom of Castile") in the 13th century promoted this effort by founding a *[Schola Traductorum](/wiki/Toledo_School_of_Translators "Toledo School of Translators")* (School of Translation) in [Toledo](/wiki/Toledo,_Spain "Toledo, Spain"). There Arabic texts, Hebrew texts, and Latin texts were translated into the other tongues by Muslim, Jewish, and Christian scholars, who also argued the merits of their respective religions. Latin translations of Greek and original Arab works of scholarship and science helped advance European [Scholasticism](/wiki/Scholasticism "Scholasticism"), and thus European science and culture.

The broad historic trends in Western translation practice may be illustrated on the example of translation into the English language.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Chaucer_Hoccleve.png/330px-Chaucer_Hoccleve.png)](/wiki/File:Chaucer_Hoccleve.png)

[Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer")

The first fine translations into English were made in the 14th century by [Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer"), who adapted from the [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") of [Giovanni Boccaccio](/wiki/Giovanni_Boccaccio "Giovanni Boccaccio") in his own *[Knight's Tale](/wiki/Knight%27s_Tale "Knight's Tale")* and *[Troilus and Criseyde](/wiki/Troilus_and_Criseyde "Troilus and Criseyde")*, began a translation of the French-language *[Roman de la Rose](/wiki/Roman_de_la_Rose "Roman de la Rose")*, and completed a translation of Boethius from the Latin. Chaucer founded an English [poetic](/wiki/Poetic "Poetic") tradition on [adaptations](/wiki/Literary_adaptation "Literary adaptation") and translations from those earlier-established [literary languages](/wiki/Literary_language "Literary language").[[105]](#cite_note-Cohen13-113)

The first great English translation was the [Wycliffe Bible](/wiki/Wycliffe_Bible "Wycliffe Bible") (c. 1382), which showed the weaknesses of an underdeveloped English [prose](/wiki/Prose "Prose"). Only at the end of the 15th century did the great age of English prose translation begin with [Thomas Malory](/wiki/Thomas_Malory "Thomas Malory")'s *[Le Morte d'Arthur](/wiki/Le_Morte_d%27Arthur "Le Morte d'Arthur")*—an adaptation of [Arthurian romances](/wiki/Arthurian_romance "Arthurian romance") so free that it can, in fact, hardly be called a true translation. The first great [Tudor](/wiki/Tudor_period "Tudor period") translations are, accordingly, the [Tyndale New Testament](/wiki/Tyndale_New_Testament "Tyndale New Testament") (1525), which influenced the [Authorized Version](/wiki/Authorized_Version "Authorized Version") (1611), and [Lord Berners](/wiki/Lord_Berners "Lord Berners")' version of [Jean Froissart](/wiki/Jean_Froissart "Jean Froissart")'s *Chronicles* (1523–25).[[105]](#cite_note-Cohen13-113)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/2a/Portrait_of_Marsilio_Ficino_at_the_Duomo_Firence_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Marsilio_Ficino_at_the_Duomo_Firence_2.jpg)

[Marsilio Ficino](/wiki/Marsilio_Ficino "Marsilio Ficino")

Meanwhile, in [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), a new period in the history of translation had opened in [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence") with the arrival, at the court of [Cosimo de' Medici](/wiki/Cosimo_de%27_Medici "Cosimo de' Medici"), of the [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") scholar [Georgius Gemistus Pletho](/wiki/Georgius_Gemistus_Pletho "Georgius Gemistus Pletho") shortly before the fall of [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") to the Turks (1453). A Latin translation of [Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato")'s works was undertaken by [Marsilio Ficino](/wiki/Marsilio_Ficino "Marsilio Ficino"). This and [Erasmus](/wiki/Erasmus "Erasmus")' Latin edition of the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") led to a new attitude to translation. For the first time, readers demanded rigor of rendering, as philosophical and religious beliefs depended on the exact words of Plato, [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") and [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus").[[105]](#cite_note-Cohen13-113)

Non-scholarly literature, however, continued to rely on *adaptation*. [France](/wiki/France "France")'s *[Pléiade](/wiki/Pl%C3%A9iade "Pléiade")*, England's Tudor poets, and the [Elizabethan](/wiki/Elizabethan "Elizabethan") translators adapted themes by [Horace](/wiki/Horace "Horace"), [Ovid](/wiki/Ovid "Ovid"), [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch") and modern Latin writers, forming a new poetic style on those models. The English poets and translators sought to supply a new public, created by the rise of a [middle class](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class") and the development of [printing](/wiki/Printing "Printing"), with works such as the original authors *would have written*, had they been writing in England in that day.[[105]](#cite_note-Cohen13-113)

The Elizabethan period of translation saw considerable progress beyond mere paraphrase toward an ideal of [stylistic](/wiki/Stylistics_(linguistics) "Stylistics (linguistics)") equivalence, but even to the end of this period, which actually reached to the middle of the 17th century, there was no concern for [verbal](/wiki/Words "Words") [accuracy](/wiki/Accuracy "Accuracy").[[106]](#cite_note-Cohen14-114)

In the second half of the 17th century, the poet John Dryden sought to make [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil") speak "in words such as he would probably have written if he were living and an Englishman". As great as Dryden's poem is, however, one is reading Dryden, and not experiencing the Roman poet's concision. Similarly, [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer") arguably suffers from [Alexander Pope](/wiki/Alexander_Pope "Alexander Pope")'s endeavor to reduce the Greek poet's "wild paradise" to order. Both works live on as worthy *English* epics, more than as a point of access to the Latin or Greek.[[106]](#cite_note-Cohen14-114)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Edward_FitzGerald.jpg/250px-Edward_FitzGerald.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edward_FitzGerald.jpg)

[Edward FitzGerald](/wiki/Edward_FitzGerald_(poet) "Edward FitzGerald (poet)")

Throughout the 18th century, the watchword of translators was ease of reading. Whatever they did not understand in a text, or thought might bore readers, they omitted. They cheerfully assumed that their own style of expression was the best, and that texts should be made to conform to it in translation. For scholarship they cared no more than had their predecessors, and they did not shrink from making translations from translations in third languages, or from languages that they hardly knew, or—as in the case of [James Macpherson](/wiki/James_Macpherson "James Macpherson")'s "translations" of [Ossian](/wiki/Ossian "Ossian")—from texts that were actually of the "translator's" own composition.[[106]](#cite_note-Cohen14-114)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Benjamin_Jowett_-_Imagines_philologorum.jpg/250px-Benjamin_Jowett_-_Imagines_philologorum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Benjamin_Jowett_-_Imagines_philologorum.jpg)

[Benjamin Jowett](/wiki/Benjamin_Jowett "Benjamin Jowett")

The 19th century brought new standards of accuracy and style. In regard to accuracy, observes J.M. Cohen, the policy became "the text, the whole text, and nothing but the text", except for any [bawdy](/wiki/Bawdy "Bawdy") passages and the addition of copious explanatory [footnotes](/wiki/Footnote "Footnote").[[i]](#cite_note-115) In regard to style, the [Victorians](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era")' aim, achieved through far-reaching metaphrase (literality) or *pseudo*-metaphrase, was to constantly remind readers that they were reading a *foreign* classic. An exception was the outstanding translation in this period, [Edward FitzGerald](/wiki/Edward_FitzGerald_(poet) "Edward FitzGerald (poet)")'s *[Rubaiyat](/wiki/Rubaiyat_of_Omar_Khayyam "Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam")* of [Omar Khayyam](/wiki/Omar_Khayyam "Omar Khayyam") (1859), which achieved its Oriental flavor largely by using Persian names and discreet Biblical echoes and actually drew little of its material from the Persian original.[[106]](#cite_note-Cohen14-114)

In advance of the 20th century, a new pattern was set in 1871 by [Benjamin Jowett](/wiki/Benjamin_Jowett "Benjamin Jowett"), who translated Plato into simple, straightforward language. Jowett's example was not followed, however, until well into the new century, when accuracy rather than style became the principal criterion.[[106]](#cite_note-Cohen14-114)

### Modern translation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Modern translation")]

As a language evolves, texts in an earlier version of the language—original texts, or old translations—may become difficult for modern readers to understand. Such a text may therefore be translated into more modern language, producing a "modern translation" (e.g., a "modern English translation" or "modernized translation").

Such modern rendering is applied either to literature from classical languages such as Latin or Greek, notably to the Bible (see "[Modern English Bible translations](/wiki/Modern_English_Bible_translations "Modern English Bible translations")"), or to literature from an earlier stage of the same language, as with the works of [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") (which are largely understandable by a modern audience, though with some difficulty) or with [Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer")'s [Middle-English](/wiki/Middle-English "Middle-English") *[Canterbury Tales](/wiki/Canterbury_Tales "Canterbury Tales")* (which is understandable to most modern readers only through heavy dependence on footnotes). In 2015 the [Oregon Shakespeare Festival](/wiki/Oregon_Shakespeare_Festival "Oregon Shakespeare Festival") commissioned professional translation of the entire Shakespeare canon, including disputed works such as *[Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III_(play) "Edward III (play)")*,[[107]](#cite_note-116) into contemporary vernacular English; in 2019, off-off-Broadway, the canon was premiered in a month-long series of staged readings.[[108]](#cite_note-117)

[Anna North](/wiki/Anna_North "Anna North") writes: "Translating the long-dead language [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer") used — a variant of [ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") called Homeric Greek — into contemporary English is no easy task, and translators bring their own skills, opinions, and stylistic sensibilities to the text. The result is that every translation is different, almost a new poem in itself." An example is [Emily Wilson](/wiki/Emily_Wilson_(classicist) "Emily Wilson (classicist)")'s 2017 translation of Homer's *[Odyssey](/wiki/Odyssey "Odyssey")*, where by conscious choice Wilson "lays bare the morals of its time and place, and invites us to consider how different they are from our own, and how similar."[[109]](#cite_note-118)

### Poetry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Poetry")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Hofstadter2002B.jpg/250px-Hofstadter2002B.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hofstadter2002B.jpg)

[Hofstadter](/wiki/Douglas_Hofstadter "Douglas Hofstadter")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Roman_Jakobson.jpg/250px-Roman_Jakobson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roman_Jakobson.jpg)

[Jakobson](/wiki/Roman_Jakobson "Roman Jakobson")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Vladimir_Nabokov_1973.jpg/250px-Vladimir_Nabokov_1973.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vladimir_Nabokov_1973.jpg)

[Nabokov](/wiki/Vladimir_Nabokov "Vladimir Nabokov")

Views on the possibility of satisfactorily translating poetry show a broad spectrum, depending partly on the degree of latitude desired by the translator in regard to a poem's formal features (rhythm, rhyme, verse form, etc.), but also relating to how much of the suggestiveness and imagery in the host poem can be recaptured or approximated in the target language. In his 1997 book *[Le Ton beau de Marot](/wiki/Le_Ton_beau_de_Marot "Le Ton beau de Marot")*, [Douglas Hofstadter](/wiki/Douglas_Hofstadter "Douglas Hofstadter") argued that a good translation of a poem must convey as much as possible not only of its literal meaning but also of its form and structure (meter, rhyme or alliteration scheme, etc.).[[110]](#cite_note-119)

The [Russian](/wiki/Russia "Russia")-born [linguist](/wiki/Linguist "Linguist") and [semiotician](/wiki/Semiotician "Semiotician") [Roman Jakobson](/wiki/Roman_Jakobson "Roman Jakobson"), however, had in his 1959 paper "[On Linguistic Aspects of Translation](/wiki/On_Linguistic_Aspects_of_Translation "On Linguistic Aspects of Translation")", declared that "poetry by definition [is] untranslatable". [Vladimir Nabokov](/wiki/Vladimir_Nabokov "Vladimir Nabokov"), another Russian-born author, took a view similar to Jakobson's. He considered rhymed, metrical, versed poetry to be in principle untranslatable and therefore rendered his 1964 English translation of [Alexander Pushkin](/wiki/Alexander_Pushkin "Alexander Pushkin")'s [*Eugene Onegin* in prose](/wiki/Eugene_Onegin#Into_English "Eugene Onegin").

Hofstadter, in *Le Ton beau de Marot*, criticized Nabokov's attitude toward verse translation. In 1999 Hofstadter published his own translation of *Eugene Onegin*, in verse form.

However, a number of more contemporary literary translators of poetry lean toward [Alexander von Humboldt](/wiki/Alexander_von_Humboldt "Alexander von Humboldt")'s notion of language as a "third universe" existing "midway between the phenomenal reality of the 'empirical world' and the internalized structures of consciousness."[[111]](#cite_note-120) Perhaps this is what poet [Sholeh Wolpé](/wiki/Sholeh_Wolp%C3%A9 "Sholeh Wolpé"), translator of the 12th-century Iranian epic poem *[The Conference of the Birds](/wiki/The_Conference_of_the_Birds "The Conference of the Birds")*, means when she writes:

> Twelfth-century Persian and contemporary English are as different as sky and sea. The best I can do as a poet is to reflect one into the other. The sea can reflect the sky with its moving stars, shifting clouds, gestations of the moon, and migrating birds—but ultimately the sea is not the sky. By nature, it is liquid. It ripples. There are waves. If you are a fish living in the sea, you can only understand the sky if its reflection becomes part of the water. Therefore, this translation of *The Conference of the Birds*, while faithful to the original text, aims at its re-creation into a still living and breathing work of literature.[[112]](#cite_note-121)

Poet [Sherod Santos](/wiki/Sherod_Santos "Sherod Santos") writes: "The task is not to reproduce the content, but with the flint and the steel of one's own language to spark what Robert Lowell has called 'the fire and finish of the original.'"[[113]](#cite_note-122)
According to [Walter Benjamin](/wiki/Walter_Benjamin "Walter Benjamin"):

> While a poet's words endure in his own language, even the greatest translation is destined to become part of the growth of its own language and eventually to perish with its renewal. Translation is so far removed from being the sterile equation of two dead languages that of all literary forms it is the one charged with the special mission of watching over the maturing process of the original language and the birth pangs of its own.[[114]](#cite_note-123)

Gregory Hays, in the course of discussing [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") adapted translations of [ancient Greek literature](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_literature "Ancient Greek literature"), makes approving reference to some views on the translating of poetry expressed by [David Bellos](/wiki/David_Bellos "David Bellos"), an accomplished French-to-English translator. Hays writes:

> Among the *idées reçues* [received ideas] skewered by David Bellos is the old saw that "poetry is what gets lost in translation." The saying is often attributed to [Robert Frost](/wiki/Robert_Frost "Robert Frost"), but as Bellos notes, the attribution is as dubious as the idea itself. A translation is an assemblage of words, and as such it can contain as much or as little poetry as any other such assemblage. The [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_people "Japanese people") even have a word (*chōyaku*, roughly "hypertranslation") to designate a version that deliberately improves on the original.[[115]](#cite_note-124)

The translator's task, when translating [rhymed verse](/wiki/Rhyme "Rhyme"), is more constraining than is the task of the verse's author: the author has full freedom to coordinate his thought with his words; the translator is constrained to adjusting his words to the author's thought.

### Book titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Book titles")]

Book-title translations can be either descriptive or symbolic. Descriptive book titles, for example [Antoine de Saint-Exupéry](/wiki/Antoine_de_Saint-Exup%C3%A9ry "Antoine de Saint-Exupéry")'s *[Le Petit Prince](/wiki/Le_Petit_Prince "Le Petit Prince")* (The Little Prince), are meant to be informative, and can name the protagonist, and indicate the theme of the book. An example of a symbolic book title is [Stieg Larsson](/wiki/Stieg_Larsson "Stieg Larsson")'s *[The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo](/wiki/The_Girl_with_the_Dragon_Tattoo "The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo")*, whose original Swedish title is *Män som hatar kvinnor* (Men Who Hate Women). Such symbolic book titles usually indicate the theme, issues, or atmosphere of the work.

When translators are working with long book titles, the translated titles are often shorter and indicate the theme of the book.[[116]](#cite_note-125)

### Plays

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Plays")]

The translation of plays poses many problems such as the added element of actors, speech duration, translation literalness, and the relationship between the arts of drama and acting. Successful play translators are able to create language that allows the actor and the playwright to work together effectively.[[117]](#cite_note-Carlson1964-126): 55 Play translators must also take into account several other aspects: the final performance, varying theatrical and acting traditions, characters' speaking styles, modern theatrical discourse, and even the acoustics of the auditorium, i.e., whether certain words will have the same effect on the new audience as they had on the original audience.[[118]](#cite_note-127)

Audiences in Shakespeare's time were more accustomed than modern playgoers to actors having longer stage time.[[117]](#cite_note-Carlson1964-126): 56 Modern translators tend to simplify the sentence structures of earlier dramas, which included compound sentences with intricate hierarchies of subordinate clauses.[[119]](#cite_note-128)[[120]](#cite_note-129)

### Chinese literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Chinese literature")]

In translating Chinese literature, translators struggle to find true fidelity in translating into the target language. In *The Poem Behind the Poem*, Barnstone argues that poetry "can't be made to sing through a mathematics that doesn't factor in the creativity of the translator".[[121]](#cite_note-130)

A notable piece of work translated into English is the *[Wen Xuan](/wiki/Wen_Xuan "Wen Xuan")*, an anthology representative of major works of Chinese literature. Translating this work requires a high knowledge of the [genres](/wiki/Genre "Genre") presented in the book, such as poetic forms, various prose types including memorials, letters, proclamations, praise poems, edicts, and historical, philosophical and political disquisitions, threnodies and laments for the dead, and examination essays. Thus the literary translator must be familiar with the writings, lives, and thought of a large number of its 130 authors, making the *Wen Xuan* one of the most difficult literary works to translate.[[122]](#cite_note-131)

### Religious texts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Religious texts")]

Further information: [Bible translations](/wiki/Bible_translations "Bible translations") and [Quran translations](/wiki/Quran_translations "Quran translations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Domenico_Ghirlandaio_-_St_Jerome_in_his_study.jpg/250px-Domenico_Ghirlandaio_-_St_Jerome_in_his_study.jpg)](/wiki/File:Domenico_Ghirlandaio_-_St_Jerome_in_his_study.jpg)

[Jerome](/wiki/Jerome "Jerome"), [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") of translators and [encyclopedists](/wiki/Encyclopedia "Encyclopedia")

An important role in history has been played by translation of religious texts. Such translations may be influenced by tension between the text and the religious values the translators wish to convey.[[123]](#cite_note-132) For example, [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") [monks](/wiki/Monk "Monk") who translated the [Indian](/wiki/India "India") [sutras](/wiki/Sutra "Sutra") into [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") occasionally adjusted their translations to better reflect [China](/wiki/China "China")'s distinct [culture](/wiki/Culture "Culture"), emphasizing notions such as [filial piety](/wiki/Filial_piety "Filial piety").

One of the first recorded instances of translation in the West was the 3rd century BCE rendering of some books of the biblical [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") from Hebrew into [Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek"). The translation is known as the "[Septuagint](/wiki/Septuagint "Septuagint")", a name that refers to the supposedly seventy translators (seventy-two, in some versions) who were commissioned to translate the Bible at [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), Egypt. According to legend, each translator worked in solitary confinement in his own cell, and all seventy versions proved identical. The *Septuagint* became the [source text](/wiki/Source_text "Source text") for later translations into many languages, including Latin, [Coptic](/wiki/Coptic_language "Coptic language"), [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language"), and [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_language "Georgian language").

Still considered one of the greatest translators in history, for having rendered the Bible into Latin, is [Jerome](/wiki/Jerome "Jerome") (347–420 CE), the [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") of translators. For centuries the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") used his translation (known as the [Vulgate](/wiki/Vulgate "Vulgate")), though even this translation stirred controversy. By contrast with Jerome's contemporary, [Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo") (354–430 CE), who endorsed precise translation, Jerome believed in adaptation, and sometimes invention, in order to more effectively bring across the meaning. Jerome's colorful Vulgate translation of the Bible includes some crucial instances of "overdetermination". For example, [Isaiah](/wiki/Isaiah "Isaiah")'s prophecy announcing that the Savior will be born of a virgin, uses the word '*almah*, which is also used to describe the dancing girls at [Solomon](/wiki/Solomon "Solomon")'s court, and simply means young and nubile. Jerome, writes [Marina Warner](/wiki/Marina_Warner "Marina Warner"), translates it as *virgo*, "adding divine authority to the virulent cult of [sexual](/wiki/Sex "Sex") disgust that shaped Christian moral theology (the [Moslem] *[Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran")*, free from this linguistic trap, does not connect [Mariam](/wiki/Mariam "Mariam")/[Mary](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus")'s miraculous nature with moral horror of sex)." The apple that [Eve](/wiki/Eve "Eve") offered to [Adam](/wiki/Adam "Adam"), according to Mark Polizzotti, could equally well have been an [apricot](/wiki/Apricot "Apricot"), orange, or banana; but Jerome liked the [pun](/wiki/Pun "Pun") *malus/malum* (apple/evil).[[30]](#cite_note-This_Little_Art_2018_p._22-30)

[Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis") has suggested that the phrase "lead us not into temptation", in the [Lord's Prayer](/wiki/Lord%27s_Prayer "Lord's Prayer") found in the [Gospels of Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew") (the first Gospel, written c. 80–90 CE) and [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke") (the third Gospel, written c. 80–110 CE), should more properly be translated, "do not let us fall into temptation", commenting that God does not lead people into temptation—[Satan](/wiki/Satan "Satan") does.[[j]](#cite_note-134) Some important early Christian authors interpreted the Bible's Greek text and [Jerome](/wiki/Jerome "Jerome")'s Latin Vulgate similarly to Pope Francis. A.J.B. Higgins[[125]](#cite_note-135) in 1943 showed that among the earliest Christian authors, the understanding and even the text of this devotional verse underwent considerable changes. These ancient writers suggest that, even if the Greek and Latin texts are left unmodified, something like "do not let us fall" could be an acceptable English rendering. Higgins cited [Tertullian](/wiki/Tertullian "Tertullian"), the earliest of the Latin [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers") (c. 155 – c. 240 CE, "do not allow us to be led") and [Cyprian](/wiki/Cyprian "Cyprian") (c. 200–258 CE, "do not allow us to be led into temptation"). A later author, [Ambrose](/wiki/Ambrose "Ambrose") (c. 340–397 CE), followed Cyprian's interpretation. Augustine of Hippo (354–430), familiar with Jerome's Latin Vulgate rendering, observed that "many people... say it this way: 'and do not allow us to be led into temptation.'"[[126]](#cite_note-136)

In 863 CE the brothers [Saints Cyril and Methodius](/wiki/Saints_Cyril_and_Methodius "Saints Cyril and Methodius"), the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire")'s "Apostles to the Slavs", began translating parts of the Bible into the [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic") language, using the [Glagolitic script](/wiki/Glagolitic_script "Glagolitic script") that they had devised, based on the [Greek alphabet](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet").

The periods preceding and contemporary with the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") saw translations of the Bible into [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") (local) European languages—a development that contributed to [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity")'s split into Roman Catholicism and [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") over disparities between Catholic and Protestant renderings of crucial words and passages (and due to a Protestant-perceived need to reform the Roman Catholic Church). Lasting effects on the religions, cultures, and languages of their respective countries were exerted by such Bible translations as [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther")'s into German (the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), 1522), [Jakub Wujek](/wiki/Jakub_Wujek "Jakub Wujek")'s into Polish (1599, as revised by the [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits")), and [William Tyndale's version](/wiki/Tyndale_Bible "Tyndale Bible") (New Testament, 1526 and revisions) and the [King James Version](/wiki/King_James_Version "King James Version") into English (1611).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Moses_%28Michaelangelo_-_San_Pietro_in_Vincoli_-_Rome%29.jpg/330px-Moses_%28Michaelangelo_-_San_Pietro_in_Vincoli_-_Rome%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moses_(Michaelangelo_-_San_Pietro_in_Vincoli_-_Rome).jpg)

Mistranslation: [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo")'s [horned Moses](/wiki/Horned_Moses "Horned Moses")

Efforts to translate the Bible into English had their [martyrs](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr"). [William Tyndale](/wiki/William_Tyndale "William Tyndale") (c. 1494–1536) was convicted of [heresy](/wiki/Heresy "Heresy") at [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), was strangled to death while tied at the stake, and then his dead body was burned.[[127]](#cite_note-137) Earlier, [John Wycliffe](/wiki/John_Wycliffe "John Wycliffe") (c. mid-1320s – 1384) had managed to die a natural death, but 30 years later the [Council of Constance](/wiki/Council_of_Constance "Council of Constance") in 1415 declared him a heretic and decreed that his works and earthly remains should be burned; the order, confirmed by [Pope Martin V](/wiki/Pope_Martin_V "Pope Martin V"), was carried out in 1428, and Wycliffe's corpse was exhumed and burned and the ashes cast into the [River Swift](/wiki/River_Swift "River Swift"). Debate and religious [schism](/wiki/Schism "Schism") over different translations of religious texts continue, as demonstrated by, for example, the [King James Only movement](/wiki/King_James_Only_movement "King James Only movement").

A famous *mistranslation* of a [Biblical](/wiki/Biblical "Biblical") text is the rendering of the Hebrew word קֶרֶן (*keren*), which has several meanings, as "[horn](/wiki/Horns_of_Moses "Horns of Moses")" in a context where it more plausibly means "beam of light": as a result, for centuries artists, including sculptor [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo"), have rendered [Moses the Lawgiver](/wiki/Moses "Moses") with horns growing from his forehead.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Chinese_quran.jpg/250px-Chinese_quran.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chinese_quran.jpg)

[Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") translation, verses 33–34 of *Quran'*s [*surah* (chapter) 36](/wiki/Ya_Sin "Ya Sin")

Such fallibility of the translation process has contributed to the [Islamic](/wiki/Islamic "Islamic") world's ambivalence about translating the *[Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran")* (also spelled *Koran*) from the original Arabic, as received by the prophet [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") from [Allah](/wiki/Allah "Allah") (God) through the angel [Gabriel](/wiki/Gabriel "Gabriel") incrementally between 609 and 632 CE, the year of Muhammad's death. During prayers, the *Quran*, as the miraculous and inimitable word of Allah, is recited only in Arabic. However, as of 1936, it had been translated into at least 102 languages.[[128]](#cite_note-fatani-138)

A fundamental difficulty in translating the *Quran* accurately stems from the fact that an Arabic word, like a Hebrew or Aramaic word, may have a [range of meanings](/wiki/Polysemy "Polysemy"), depending on [context](/wiki/Context_(language_use) "Context (language use)"). This is said to be a linguistic feature, particularly of all [Semitic languages](/wiki/Semitic_languages "Semitic languages"), that adds to the usual similar difficulties encountered in translating between any two languages.[[128]](#cite_note-fatani-138) There is always an element of human judgment—of interpretation—involved in understanding and translating a text. Muslims regard any translation of the *Quran* as but one possible interpretation of the [Quranic (Classical) Arabic](/wiki/Classical_Arabic "Classical Arabic") text, and not as a full equivalent of that divinely communicated original. Hence such a translation is often called an "interpretation" rather than a translation.[[129]](#cite_note-139)

To complicate matters further, as with other languages, the meanings and usages of some expressions have changed *over time*, between the Classical Arabic of the *Quran*, and modern Arabic. Thus a modern Arabic speaker may misinterpret the meaning of a word or passage in the *Quran*. Moreover, the interpretation of a Quranic passage will also depend on the historic context of Muhammad's life and of his early community. Properly researching that context requires a detailed knowledge of *[hadith](/wiki/Hadith "Hadith")* and *[sirah](/wiki/Prophetic_biography "Prophetic biography")*, which are themselves vast and complex texts. Hence, analogously to the translating of [Chinese literature](#Chinese_literature), an attempt at an accurate translation of the *Quran* requires a knowledge not only of the Arabic language and of the target language, including their respective evolutions, but also a deep understanding of the two [cultures](/wiki/Culture "Culture") involved.

### Experimental literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Experimental literature")]

Experimental literature, such as [Kathy Acker](/wiki/Kathy_Acker "Kathy Acker")’s novel *Don Quixote* (1986) and [Giannina Braschi](/wiki/Giannina_Braschi "Giannina Braschi")’s novel *[Yo-Yo Boing!](/wiki/Yo-Yo_Boing! "Yo-Yo Boing!")* (1998), features a translative writing that highlights discomforts of the interlingual and translingual encounters and literary translation as a creative practice.[[130]](#cite_note-fisher-140)[[131]](#cite_note-141) These authors weave their own translations into their texts.

Acker's [Postmodern](/wiki/Postmodern_literature "Postmodern literature") fiction both fragments and preserves the materiality of [Catullus](/wiki/Catullus "Catullus")’s Latin text in ways that tease out its semantics and syntax without wholly appropriating them, a method that unsettles the notion of any fixed and finished translation.[[130]](#cite_note-fisher-140)

Whereas Braschi's trilogy of experimental works (*[Empire of Dreams](/wiki/Empire_of_Dreams_(poetry_collection) "Empire of Dreams (poetry collection)")*, 1988; *Yo-Yo Boing!*, 1998, and *[United States of Banana](/wiki/United_States_of_Banana "United States of Banana")*, 2011) deals with the very subject of translation.[[132]](#cite_note-142) Her trilogy presents the evolution of the Spanish language through loose translations of dramatic, poetic, and philosophical writings from the Medieval, [Golden Age](/wiki/Spanish_Golden_Age "Spanish Golden Age"), and [Modernist](/wiki/Modernismo "Modernismo") eras into contemporary Caribbean, Latin American, and Nuyorican Spanish expressions. Braschi's translations of classical texts in Iberian Spanish (into other regional and historical linguistic and poetic frameworks) challenge the concept of national languages.[[133]](#cite_note-143)

### Science fiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Science fiction")]

[Science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction") being a [genre](/wiki/Genre "Genre") with a recognizable set of conventions and literary genealogies, in which language often includes [neologisms](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism"), neosemes,[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] and [invented languages](/wiki/Invented_languages "Invented languages"), techno-scientific and [pseudoscientific](/wiki/Pseudoscientific "Pseudoscientific") vocabulary,[[134]](#cite_note-144) and fictional representation of the translation process,[[135]](#cite_note-145)[[136]](#cite_note-146) the translation of science-fiction texts involves specific concerns.[[137]](#cite_note-Iannuzzi2018-147) The science-fiction translator tends to acquire specific competences and assume a distinctive publishing and cultural agency.[[138]](#cite_note-148)[[139]](#cite_note-149) As in the case of other mass-fiction genres, this professional specialization and role often is not recognized by publishers and scholars.[[140]](#cite_note-150)

Translation of science fiction accounts for the transnational nature of science fiction's repertoire of shared conventions and [tropes](/wiki/Trope_(literature) "Trope (literature)"). After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), many European countries were swept by a wave of translations from the English.[[141]](#cite_note-151)[[142]](#cite_note-152) Due to the prominence of English as a source language, the use of [pseudonyms](/wiki/Pseudonym "Pseudonym") and [pseudotranslations](/wiki/Pseudotranslation "Pseudotranslation") became common in countries such as Italy[[137]](#cite_note-Iannuzzi2018-147) and Hungary,[[143]](#cite_note-153) and English has often been used as a [vehicular language](/wiki/Vehicular_language "Vehicular language") to translate from languages such as Chinese and Japanese.[[144]](#cite_note-Iannuzzi-154)

More recently, the international market in science-fiction translations has seen an increasing presence of source languages other than English.[[144]](#cite_note-Iannuzzi-154)

## Technical translation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Technical translation")]

Main article: [Technical translation](/wiki/Technical_translation "Technical translation")

Technical translation renders documents whose useful lives are often limited – such as manuals, instruction sheets, internal memos, minutes, and financial reports – for a limited audience who are directly affected by the document. Thus, a user guide for a particular model of refrigerator is useful only for the refrigerator's owner and will remain useful only so long as that refrigerator model is in use. Similarly, software documentation generally pertains to a particular software, whose applications are used only by a certain class of users.[[145]](#cite_note-155)

Some translators need to entrust letters, debates, and similar texts in other languages and specialized fields to other translators in order to enhance the completeness of their work. For example, in the book *[Tarikh-e Alam-ara-ye Abbasi](/wiki/Tarikh-e_Alam-ara-ye_Abbasi "Tarikh-e Alam-ara-ye Abbasi")* the translator collaborated with an Ottoman Turkish translator and a specialist in Islamic sciences to translate the work into English.[[146]](#cite_note-Munshi-156) Some translators also need to travel to different countries for accurate translation and identification of geographical names. They sometimes seek assistance from specialists to read and translate certain difficult and illegible historical texts.[[146]](#cite_note-Munshi-156)

## Survey translation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Survey translation")]

A [survey](/wiki/Survey_(human_research) "Survey (human research)") [questionnaire](/wiki/Questionnaire "Questionnaire") consists of a list of questions and answer categories aimed at extracting data from a particular group of people about their attitude, behavior, or knowledge. In cross-national and cross-cultural [survey research](/wiki/Survey_methodology "Survey methodology"), translation is crucial to collecting comparable data.[[147]](#cite_note-157)[[148]](#cite_note-158) Originally developed for the [European Social Surveys](/wiki/European_Social_Survey "European Social Survey"), the model TRAPD (Translation, Review, Adjudication, Pretest, and Documentation) is now "widely used in the global survey research community, although not always labeled as such or implemented in its complete form".[[149]](#cite_note-159)[[150]](#cite_note-160)[[151]](#cite_note-:02-161)

A team approach is recommended in the survey-translation process, to include translators, subject-matter experts, and persons helpful to the process.[[152]](#cite_note-162) For example, even when project managers and researchers do not speak the language of the translation, they know the study objectives well and the intent behind the questions, and therefore have a key role in improving the translation.[[153]](#cite_note-163) In addition, a survey-translation framework based on [sociolinguistics](/wiki/Sociolinguistics "Sociolinguistics") states that a linguistically appropriate translation cannot be wholly sufficient to achieve the communicative effect of the source-language survey; the translation must also incorporate the social practices and cultural norms of the target language.[[154]](#cite_note-164)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-32)** French philosopher and writer [Gilles Ménage](/wiki/Gilles_M%C3%A9nage "Gilles Ménage") (1613-92) commented on translations by humanist Perrot Nicolas d'Ablancourt (1606-64): "They remind me of a woman whom I greatly loved in [Tours](/wiki/Tours "Tours"), who was beautiful but unfaithful."[[31]](#cite_note-31)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-60)** Cf. a supposed comment by [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"): "This is the type of pedantry up with which I will not put."
3. **[^](#cite_ref-66)** "Interpretation" in this sense is to be distinguished from the function of an "[interpreter](#Interpreting)" who translates orally or by the use of [sign language](/wiki/Sign_language "Sign language").
4. **[^](#cite_ref-68)** Rebecca Armstrong writes: "A translator has to make choices; any word they choose will carry its own nuance, a particular set of interpretations, implications and associations. [Often the translator] need[s] to render the same [...] word differently in different contexts."[[64]](#cite_note-67)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-74)** See "[Poetry](#Poetry)", below, for a similar observation concerning the occasional superiority of the translation over the original.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-86)** Elsewhere Merwin recalls Pound saying: "[A]t your age you don't have anything to write about. You may think you do, but you don't. So get to work translating. The [Provençal](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language") is the real source...."[[80]](#cite_note-85)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-89)** For example, in [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), a "translation" is "*przekład*" or "*tłumaczenie*." Both "translator" and "interpreter" are "*tłumacz*." For a time in the 18th century, however, for "translator," some writers used a word, "*przekładowca*," that is no longer in use.[[82]](#cite_note-88)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-101)** J.M. Cohen observes: "Scientific translation is the aim of an age that would reduce all activities to [techniques](/wiki/Technology "Technology"). It is impossible however to imagine a literary-translation machine less complex than the human brain itself, with all its knowledge, reading, and discrimination."[[93]](#cite_note-100)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-115)** For instance, Henry Benedict Mackey's translation of [St. Francis de Sales](/wiki/St._Francis_de_Sales "St. Francis de Sales")'s "[Treatise on the Love of God](http://www.ccel.org/ccel/desales/love.html)" consistently omits the saint's analogies comparing God to a nursing mother, references to Bible stories such as the rape of Tamar, and so forth.
10. **[^](#cite_ref-134)** MJC Warren, Lecturer in Biblical and Religious Studies, [University of Sheffield](/wiki/University_of_Sheffield "University of Sheffield"), points out (more explicitly than Charles McNamara) that Luke gives a shorter version of Jesus's Lord's Prayer, leaving off the request that God "deliver us from evil"; that (as Charles McNamara also says) accurate translation is not the question here; and that the Bible records a number of incidents when God commands evil actions, such as that [Abraham](/wiki/Abraham "Abraham") kill his only son, [Isaac](/wiki/Isaac "Isaac") (whose execution is canceled at the last moment).[[124]](#cite_note-133)

"Historians have fastened their attention on the letters that passed from [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") to the [Qianlong emperor](/wiki/Qianlong_emperor "Qianlong emperor") and back again. But ... written texts are not so fixed as one might assume. Neither the Chinese nor the British officials read the originals of the messages from the other side; they were content to receive translations... In such circumstances ... meanings become elusive. More than king, emperor or ambassador, the translators decided the substance of the exchange. Historians have tended to attribute meaning to the speakers and not to their humble interpreters. But ... it was the intermediaries – ambassadors, negotiators, translators – who delivered the meanings. The important persons in this process were those in between." (p. 32.)
